title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
This is the foundation document for the Competencies category. It explains what competency-based (also called behavioural or structured) interviewing is, summarises the published evidence for why employers across the UK and EU use it, and organises the competencies that recur across major frameworks into eight recurring **competency families**: working with others; communication and influencing; leadership and management; problem-solving and analysis; delivering results; resilience and adaptability; integrity and values; and customer and stakeholder focus. It then maps those families onto three named public frameworks — the UK Civil Service Success Profiles Behaviours [2], the EPSO general competencies used for EU-institution selection [4], and the NHS values [6] — and onto the competency labels most commonly assessed in private-sector and graduate-scheme recruitment [15].

The document serves both audiences of this knowledge base. Candidates learn how to recognise which competency a question is really testing from its wording, so preparation effort lands where it counts. Interviewers and HR practitioners see how the major frameworks define and group competencies, and why professional-body and academic evidence favours structured, criterion-scored interviewing over unstructured conversation [12] [18].

Document-level confidence is **Medium**. The framework definitions, competency lists and validity evidence rest on official and academic sources and would individually merit High confidence; however, the eight-family taxonomy and the cross-framework mapping table are this knowledge base's own reasoned synthesis (clearly labelled as such), and a document whose central organising device is synthesis is rated Medium under the confidence rubric.
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
- Competency-based interviews ask candidates for real past examples ("Tell me about a time when…") and score the answers against pre-agreed criteria; the CIPD describes structured interviews as asking a predefined set of questions, in the same order, to all candidates, with responses scored against consistent criteria. *(documented)* [12]
- The evidence case is strong: in the most recent large re-analysis of selection-method validity, structured interviews emerged as the top-ranked selection procedure for predicting job performance. *(documented)* [18] [19]
- The UK Civil Service assesses nine named Behaviours (e.g. Working Together, Communicating and Influencing, Delivering at Pace) as part of Success Profiles [1] [2]; EPSO assesses eight general competencies (e.g. Working Together, Communication, Decision-Making and Getting Results) defined in its 2023 Competency Framework [3] [4]. *(documented)*
- The same underlying families recur across public-sector, EU and graduate-scheme frameworks under different names — a candidate who prepares one strong example per family can adapt it across employers. *(guidance)*
- Question wording signals the competency being tested: stems such as "worked in a team", "persuade/explain", "led", "difficult decision", "tight deadline", "setback/pressure" each point to a different family. *(guidance, illustrated with sourced questions below)*
- Interviewers should ask each applicant the same questions where possible and base decisions on the job description and person specification — this is ACAS guidance and also reduces discrimination risk under the Equality Act 2010. *(documented)* [7] [11]
- Graduate-scheme competition is intense — ISE data reported an average of 140 applications per graduate job in 2024 — which is one reason large employers rely on structured, criterion-scored assessment at scale. *(documented)* [17]
- The eight-family taxonomy used in this knowledge base is an editorial organising device, not an official standard; always check the named framework an employer actually uses. *(guidance)*
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text: This document answers two questions. For candidates: *"What competencies do UK and EU employers actually assess in interviews, and how do I tell which one a question is testing?"* For interviewers and HR: *"What are the recognised competency families, how do the major frameworks define them, and what does good evidence-based practice look like when mapping questions to competencies?"* It is the parent document for the per-competency deep-dives in this category, which take each family to depth; this document deliberately stays broad.
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
**Covered:** the definition and evidence base of competency-based/structured interviewing; eight recurring competency families; mapping of those families onto the UK Civil Service Success Profiles Behaviours, the EPSO general competencies, NHS values (briefly), and common private-sector/graduate-scheme competency labels; how question wording signals the competency assessed; a starter question bank spanning the families; and the legal and fairness essentials for UK and EU markets.

**Not covered (deliberate exclusions):** per-competency depth — level descriptors, grade-by-grade expectations and extended model answers belong to the child documents (teamwork-collaboration, communication-influencing, leadership-management, problem-solving-decision-making, delivering-results-planning, resilience-adaptability, integrity-values-ethics, customer-stakeholder-service, all planned); detailed treatment of interview techniques such as STAR/CARL (see techniques-foundation); full framework guides (see uk-frameworks-foundation and eu-frameworks-foundation); and interview formats such as panels, video and assessment centres (see formats-foundation). Sector-specific frameworks (e.g. policing, teaching) are out of scope here.
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
- **Competency / behaviour** — in the Civil Service Success Profiles framework, Behaviours are "the actions and activities that people do which result in effective performance in a job" [2]. "Competency" is the more common generic term for the same idea in private-sector and EU usage [3] [15].
- **Competency-based (behavioural) interview** — an interview in which candidates draw on examples from their own experience to demonstrate particular competencies; targetjobs describes it as one where "recruiters will ask questions where you will need to draw on examples from your life so far to demonstrate times when you have employed particular competencies" [15].
- **Structured interview** — per CIPD guidance, an interview using a predefined set of questions, asked in the same order to all candidates, with responses scored using consistent criteria against pre-agreed criteria for each question [12].
- **Anchor** — in the EPSO Competency Framework, an observable element of a general competency used to recognise and assess it [4].
- **Competency family** — this knowledge base's term for a cluster of closely related competencies that recur across frameworks under different names. An editorial grouping, not an official framework term.
- **STAR** — Situation, Task, Action, Result: a widely recommended structure for competency answers [13]. Treated fully in techniques-foundation.
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

## UK Civil Service: Success Profiles Behaviours

Success Profiles are the recruitment framework used within the UK Civil Service. A Success Profile can draw on five elements — Behaviours, Strengths, Ability, Experience and Technical — with the mix depending on the role [1]. The Behaviours element defines nine named behaviours [2]:

1. Seeing the Big Picture
2. Changing and Improving
3. Making Effective Decisions
4. Leadership
5. Communicating and Influencing
6. Working Together
7. Developing Self and Others
8. Managing a Quality Service
9. Delivering at Pace

Behaviours are assessed in several ways across the recruitment process, including written examples at application, interviews, and job-related simulations; candidates may be asked to give examples of when they have demonstrated a particular behaviour [2].

## EU institutions: EPSO general competencies

The European Personnel Selection Office (EPSO) selects staff for the EU institutions against a Competency Framework that "defines the competencies that are considered essential to perform effectively in any given job" [3]. The framework in use since 2023 defines eight general competencies, each broken into observable anchors [4]:

1. Critical thinking, analysing & creative problem-solving
2. Decision-making & getting results
3. Information management (digital and data literacy)
4. Self-management
5. Working together
6. Learning as a skill
7. Communication
8. Intrapreneurship

For any given competition, the Notice of Competition published in the Official Journal is the only official document containing detailed information about the actual tests used [5].

## NHS (brief)

NHS recruitment in England sits within the values of the NHS Constitution: working together for patients; respect and dignity; commitment to quality of care; compassion; improving lives; and everyone counts [6]. Values-based recruitment against these is treated in the planned NHS framework documents; they are included here because they show the same families appearing as *values* rather than *competencies*.

## Private sector and graduate schemes

There is no single private-sector framework, but the competency labels used in graduate and professional recruitment are highly convergent: targetjobs lists communication, teamwork, problem-solving, leadership, time management, planning and organisation, customer-facing skills, influencing, decision making, motivation, adaptability, commercial awareness and creativity among the competencies graduate employers commonly assess [15]. The scale of graduate recruitment pushes employers towards structured methods: Institute of Student Employers survey data reported by Prospects Luminate found the average employer received 140 applications per graduate job in 2024, a 59% increase on the prior year, across a survey of 145 employers [17].
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

## Why employers use competency-based, structured interviews

The professional-body position is clear: CIPD guidance states that structured interviews are better at predicting job performance than unstructured interviews, and that situational and past-behavioural questions are the best kinds of interview questions; consistent questions and objective scoring criteria make responses comparable and reduce the influence of personal bias [12].

The academic evidence supports this. Sackett, Zhang, Berry and Lievens (2022) re-analysed the meta-analytic validity estimates for personnel-selection methods, correcting a systematic overcorrection for range restriction; in their revised estimates, structured interviews emerged as the top-ranked selection procedure, even though absolute validity values for most methods came down from earlier estimates [18]. Their 2023 follow-up draws out the applied implications for selection-system design, including the trade-off between validity and diversity when combining predictors [19].

ACAS guidance to employers reinforces the fairness rationale: prepare questions in advance, ask each applicant the same questions where possible to ensure applicants are treated the same way, use more than one interviewer to reduce the risk of discriminating, and base decisions on how each applicant met each point in the job description and person specification [11].

## The eight competency families and how frameworks name them

> **Labelling note (synthesis):** the eight families below are this knowledge base's editorial grouping of competencies that recur across the cited frameworks. The framework entries in each row are documented [2] [4] [6] [15]; the *grouping* of them into a family is reasoned synthesis, and cells marked "—" mean the framework has no close named equivalent.

| Competency family | Civil Service Behaviours [2] | EPSO general competencies [4] | NHS values [6] | Common graduate-scheme labels [15] |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Working with others | Working Together | Working together | Working together for patients | Teamwork |
| Communication & influencing | Communicating and Influencing | Communication | — | Communication; influencing |
| Leadership & management | Leadership; Developing Self and Others | Intrapreneurship | — | Leadership; motivation; delegation |
| Problem-solving & analysis | Making Effective Decisions; Seeing the Big Picture | Critical thinking, analysing & creative problem-solving | — | Problem-solving; decision making; creativity; commercial awareness |
| Delivering results | Delivering at Pace; Managing a Quality Service | Decision-making & getting results | Commitment to quality of care | Time management; planning and organisation |
| Resilience & adaptability | Changing and Improving | Self-management; Learning as a skill | — | Adaptability |
| Integrity & values | — (values sit in the Civil Service Code, outside the Behaviours list) | — (partially visible in anchors, e.g. treating others "equally and with fairness") | Respect and dignity; compassion; everyone counts | Motivation; values fit |
| Customer & stakeholder focus | Managing a Quality Service | — | Working together for patients; improving lives | Customer-facing skills |

Documented definitions anchoring the table:

- Civil Service **Working Together**: "Form effective partnerships and relationships with people both internally and externally, from a range of diverse backgrounds, sharing information, resources and support" [2].
- Civil Service **Communicating and Influencing**: "Communicate purpose and direction with clarity, integrity and enthusiasm. Respect the needs, responses and opinions of others" [2].
- EPSO **Working together**: "Demonstrates cross-functional cooperation and fosters diversity and inclusion. Cooperates well by seeking compromise, sharing knowledge and assisting others" [4].
- EPSO **Communication**: "Conveys information and opinions clearly, generates buy-in by facilitating interaction and engaging effectively with others" [4].
- EPSO **Critical thinking, analysing & creative problem-solving**: "Thinks holistically and translates complex information into creative and innovative solutions" [4].
- EPSO **Decision-making and getting results**: "Takes decisions in a shifting environment, translates them into action and works towards tangible results" [4].
- EPSO **Self-management**: "Organises own tasks, demonstrates responsibility and a constructive attitude at all times", with anchors including responding calmly to obstacles and adapting to changing work conditions [4].
- EPSO **Intrapreneurship**: "Drives change, inspires and empowers others in implementing change and offers guidance at all levels" [4].

## How question wording signals the competency

Competency questions characteristically open with stems that invite a specific past example — targetjobs and Skills Development Scotland both present competency interviewing as asking how you have used specific skills in past situations [15] [16], and the National Careers Service presents the STAR structure against exactly such prompts (e.g. "Tell me about a time when you have shown leadership skills.") [13]. Published question sets pair recognisable wording cues with the competency assessed: for example, My World of Work maps "Describe a time you collaborated successfully with a team." to teamwork, "Name a time you had to make a challenging decision in the workplace." to decisiveness, and "Can you tell us about a time you had to work under pressure?" to resilience [16]; targetjobs maps "Can you give me an example of when you worked in a team?" to teamwork and "Tell me about a time when you took on a leadership role" to leadership [15]. The signal-reading skill this implies — keyword in the stem → family → prepared example — is developed in the coaching sections below.

text:
A starter bank spanning the eight families. Provenance per entry: *(sourced …)* questions are quoted from the named published source; *(composed)* questions were written for this knowledge base in the style of the cited frameworks and are not attributed to any employer.

## Working with others

1. Tell me about a time you have worked as part of a team? *(sourced: National Careers Service, The STAR method)* [13]
2. Describe a time you collaborated successfully with a team. *(sourced: My World of Work, Skills Development Scotland)* [16]
3. Describe a situation where you had to build a working relationship with a colleague whose approach was very different from yours. *(composed)*

## Communication & influencing

1. Have you ever had to explain a topic to somebody with limited understanding? *(sourced: targetjobs)* [15]
2. Describe a time you had to persuade a sceptical colleague or stakeholder to accept your recommendation. *(composed)*

## Leadership & management

1. Tell me about a time when you have shown leadership skills. *(sourced: National Careers Service, The STAR method)* [13]
2. Tell me about a time when you took on a leadership role. *(sourced: targetjobs)* [15]
3. Could you tell us about a time you led a complex project? How did you handle it? *(sourced: My World of Work, Skills Development Scotland)* [16]

## Problem-solving & analysis

1. Give me an example of when you faced a problem at work. How did you handle it? *(sourced: National Careers Service, The STAR method)* [13]
2. Name a time you had to make a challenging decision in the workplace. *(sourced: My World of Work, Skills Development Scotland)* [16]
3. Walk me through a time you had to reach a decision with incomplete information. What did you weigh up? *(composed)*

## Delivering results

1. Give an example of a time when you showed initiative. *(sourced: targetjobs)* [15]
2. Tell me about a time you delivered a piece of work to a tight deadline while other priorities competed for your attention. *(composed)*

## Resilience & adaptability

1. Can you tell us about a time you had to work under pressure? *(sourced: My World of Work, Skills Development Scotland)* [16]
2. Give an example of a time when you handled a major crisis. *(sourced: targetjobs)* [15]
3. Tell me about a time a project changed direction late on. How did you adapt your plans? *(composed)*

## Integrity & values

1. Tell me about a time you were asked to do something you believed was wrong. What did you do? *(composed)*
2. Describe a situation where you saw behaviour that fell short of your organisation's values. How did you respond? *(composed)*

## Customer & stakeholder focus

1. Describe a time you had to handle a customer's difficult request. *(sourced: My World of Work, Skills Development Scotland)* [16]
2. Describe a time you improved a service or product based on feedback from a customer or service user. *(composed)*

text:
**Coaching layer — this is guidance, not documented fact.**

- **Structure every example with STAR.** The National Careers Service defines the structure: the Situation you had to deal with, the Task you were given, the Action you took, and the Result — what happened and what you learned [13]. Keep Situation and Task brief; the scored substance is almost always in Action and Result.
- **Prepare one strong, recent example per family, not per question.** Because the same eight families recur across frameworks (see the mapping table above), a bank of six to eight well-rehearsed examples can be adapted to most competency interviews. Choose examples where *you* acted — "I" not "we" — since assessors score your behaviour, not your team's.
- **Match the example's altitude to the role.** A team-leading example serves the leadership family; a peer-persuasion example serves communication and influencing. Read the framework the employer names in the advert (Civil Service adverts name the Behaviours assessed [2]; EPSO notices specify the competencies tested [5]) and select examples that reach the level described.
- **Signal-read the question before answering.** Identify the keyword in the stem (team / explain / led / decision / deadline / pressure / customer), place it in a family, and retrieve the matching prepared example. If a question could fit two families (e.g. "led a project under pressure"), let the interviewer's framing decide which to foreground.
- **Worked skeleton (communication & influencing, composed question 2 above):** Situation — a stakeholder resisted a process change; Task — you needed their sign-off by month-end; Action — you sought their objections first, quantified the benefit in their terms, and offered a pilot; Result — sign-off achieved, pilot adopted. This mirrors the documented definition of Communicating and Influencing — clarity of purpose while respecting "the needs, responses and opinions of others" [2] — and the EPSO Communication anchors on tailoring the message and using convincing arguments [4].
- **End on the Result and, where natural, the learning.** The NCS formulation explicitly includes "what you learned" in the R [13]; a sentence of reflection also serves frameworks that assess learning (EPSO's Learning as a skill [4]).
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

- **What is scored.** In the Civil Service, Behaviours are assessed through written examples, tests, interviews and job simulations; candidates may be asked to give examples of when they have demonstrated a particular behaviour [2]. In EPSO competitions, competencies from the Competency Framework are assessed through the tests specified in the Notice of Competition, which is the only official document detailing the tests for a given competition [5].
- **How scoring is structured.** CIPD describes the structured method: a predefined set of questions asked in the same order to all candidates, with responses scored using consistent criteria against pre-agreed criteria for each question; CIPD also reports evidence that scoring rubrics improve fairness of outcomes [12].
- **Consistency and panel composition.** ACAS advises employers to prepare questions in advance, ask each applicant the same questions where possible so applicants are treated in the same way, and use more than one interviewer to reduce the risk of discriminating; selection decisions should be based on how each applicant met each point in the job description and person specification [11].
- **Observable anchors.** The EPSO framework shows what "evidence" means to an assessor: each competency is decomposed into observable anchors — e.g. for Working together, "Discusses differences of opinion openly with the individuals concerned … and strives for compromise in situations of conflict" [4]. Assessors listen for behaviour matching the anchors, not for opinions or hypotheticals.
- **Why the method is trusted.** The evidence ranking matters to assessors as well as candidates: structured interviews emerged as the top-ranked selection procedure in the revised meta-analytic estimates [18], and the applied literature discusses combining them with other predictors in system design [19].
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
**Coaching layer — this is guidance, not documented fact.**

- **Answering the wrong family.** Giving a teamwork story to a leadership question wastes the scoring opportunity: assessors score against the criteria for the competency asked (see the structured-scoring practice documented by CIPD [12]). Signal-read first.
- **"We" answers.** Describing the team's achievement without isolating your own actions gives the assessor no behaviour to score against anchors of the kind EPSO publishes [4].
- **Hypotheticals instead of examples.** Competency questions ask for what you *did*; frameworks explicitly ask candidates for examples of when they demonstrated the behaviour [2]. "I would…" answers fail that test unless the interview is deliberately situational.
- **One mega-example for everything.** Reusing a single story across all questions undercuts breadth; the family taxonomy exists precisely because employers sample across distinct behaviours [2] [4].
- **Result-free stories.** Stopping at Action drops the R that the STAR structure and assessors expect [13].
- **For interviewers: improvising questions per candidate.** Varying questions between candidates undermines both comparability and legal defensibility — ACAS advises the same questions where possible and warns that questions must not discriminate [11].
- **For interviewers: labelling a question with one competency and scoring another.** If the advert names the Behaviour assessed [2], score that Behaviour; drifting criteria reintroduces the unstructured-interview weaknesses documented by CIPD [12].
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
**Coaching layer.**

1. **Build the example bank (60–90 minutes).** Draw the eight-family table and write one candidate example per row from your last two to three years. Mark rows where your example is weak — those are your preparation priorities.
2. **Signal-reading drill (15 minutes).** Take the 19 questions in the Question Bank above in random order and, before answering, name the family each belongs to. Check yourself against the sub-headings.
3. **Timed STAR practice (3 × 10 minutes).** Answer one sourced question aloud in under three minutes using STAR [13]. Record yourself; check that Action and Result took at least two-thirds of the time.
4. **Anchor self-scoring (30 minutes).** Take your Working-with-others example and score it against the published EPSO Working together anchors [4]: did you show compromise-seeking, knowledge-sharing, assisting others? Rewrite the Action section to evidence at least two anchors.
5. **Interviewer drill (pairs, 30 minutes).** Draft three questions for one family, agree scoring criteria *before* the mock interview (per CIPD structured practice [12]), interview your partner, then compare the scores you each expected. Disagreements reveal vague criteria.
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited. Signposting only — not legal advice.**

- **UK — Equality Act 2010.** Part 5 of the Act covers work, including recruitment [7]. Section 4 lists nine protected characteristics: age, disability, gender reassignment, marriage and civil partnership, pregnancy and maternity, race, religion or belief, sex and sexual orientation [8]. ACAS guidance operationalises this for interviews: questions must not discriminate, and employers must ask anyone coming to an interview to say if they need reasonable adjustments to attend — for example wheelchair access, extra time for tests, or a sign-language interpreter [11].
- **EU — equal treatment in selection.** Council Directive 2000/78/EC establishes a general framework for equal treatment in employment and occupation, expressly covering "conditions for access to employment … including selection criteria and recruitment conditions", and prohibits direct and indirect discrimination on grounds of religion or belief, disability, age or sexual orientation, with reasonable-accommodation obligations for disabled persons [9].
- **Candidate data.** Interview notes, scores and application records about identifiable candidates are personal data; in the EU their processing falls under Regulation (EU) 2016/679 (GDPR) [10]. Interviewers should assume candidates may exercise data-subject rights over interview records.
- **Fairness by design.** The structured method itself is a fairness control: consistent questions and criterion-based scoring make responses comparable and reduce the scope for bias, per CIPD [12] and ACAS [11].
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
- **The family taxonomy is editorial.** The eight families are a synthesis for navigation and preparation; employers assess against their *own* named framework, and mapping cells marked "—" show real gaps. Always defer to the employer's published framework.
- **Frameworks change.** The EPSO framework in this document dates from 2023 [4], and EPSO has been through an organisational transformation; the Success Profiles guidance is periodically updated on GOV.UK [1]. Verify against the live pages before high-stakes use; review is due 2027-07-19.
- **Validity estimates are contested in magnitude.** Sackett et al. revised headline validity numbers downwards relative to older meta-analyses [18] [19]; older sources quoting higher coefficients are still widely circulated. The *ranking* of structured interviews is robust in the cited work; specific coefficients should be quoted from [18] only.
- **Careers-site question lists are practitioner sources.** The sourced questions from targetjobs and My World of Work [15] [16] evidence *what is published as typical*, not what any specific employer asks.
- **ISE figures are reported second-hand.** The 140-applications-per-job figure is ISE survey data reported via Prospects Luminate by an ISE author [17]; the underlying ISE report is member-gated and was not independently inspected.
title: Competency-Based Interviewing: Competency Families & Question Mapping

text:
- **Private-sector frameworks by name.** This document evidences graduate-scheme competency labels via careers sources [15]; obtaining named, publicly documented corporate frameworks (e.g. published bank or consultancy competency dictionaries) would strengthen the mapping table. Candidate for the per-competency child documents.
- **NHS values-based recruitment mechanics.** How NHS values [6] translate into interview questions and scoring is only signposted here; the planned NHS/UK-frameworks documents should cover values-based recruitment guidance from NHS Employers.
- **Strengths vs competency mix in graduate selection.** ISE members-only data likely quantifies the strengths-based shift in graduate recruitment; a public, citable figure has not yet been found.
- **Civil Service assessor scoring scales.** Published grade-level rating scales for Behaviours (e.g. 1–7 scales in departmental guidance) exist in departmental candidate packs; verifying a stable official URL is left to the uk-frameworks deep-dive.

title: Answer Frameworks & Techniques: STAR and Beyond

text:
This is the foundation document for the Techniques category. It maps the answer
structures that candidates are advised to use in competency-based (behavioural)
interviews across the UK and EU markets, and — the mirror image — what those
structures give an assessor to score. The anchor technique is STAR (Situation,
Task, Action, Result), which is explicitly recommended by the UK Civil
Service's own candidate guidance and by the National Careers Service, and is
the default recommendation of most university careers services. Around STAR
sits a family of documented variants: CAR (Context, Action, Results — used by
the University of Cambridge Careers Service), STARR/STAR(R) with an added
Reflection step (University of Reading, Arden University, and graduate
employers via targetjobs), CARL (Context, Action, Results, Learning —
documented in the University of Edinburgh Reflection Toolkit as an adaptation
of an interview technique), and SOAR (Situation, Obstacle, Action, Result),
which so far is documented mainly in practitioner careers writing rather than
official guidance.

The document serves both audiences. For candidates it records who recommends
which structure, what each element is for, and how to select evidence from
work, study, volunteering and life outside work. For interviewers it records
the documented link between structure and scoring: structured interviews with
consistent questions and criteria are the professional-body recommendation
(CIPD, ACAS), and the official guidance is explicit that behaviours are scored
on what the candidate did — which is precisely what the Action element of any
of these frameworks surfaces. It also records the documented boundary of the
technique: UK Civil Service strengths questions are expressly *not* to be
answered with rehearsed, pre-structured examples.

Confidence is **Medium**: the STAR recommendations and the assessment-process
facts rest on official UK government, EPSO, professional-body and statutory
sources, but the documentation of the alternative structures rests on
university careers services and named practitioner sources, and some inference
is involved in comparing them. Deep-dives on STAR itself, on the alternative
frameworks, and on building an evidence bank are planned as child documents.
title: Answer Frameworks & Techniques: STAR and Beyond

text:
- STAR (Situation, Task, Action, Result) is the answer structure named in UK
  Civil Service candidate guidance and by the National Careers Service for
  competency/behaviour questions, on application forms as well as at interview.
  *(documented)*
- Documented alternatives exist and are close cousins of STAR: CAR (Cambridge),
  STARR/STAR(R) adding Reflection (Reading, Arden, graduate employers via
  targetjobs), CARL adding Learning (Edinburgh), and SOAR replacing Task with
  Obstacle (practitioner sources only). *(documented)*
- EPSO's competency-based interview is structured — questions are designed so
  assessors can evaluate selected general competencies, about 10 minutes per
  competency — but the EPSO pages reviewed do not prescribe a named answer
  mnemonic. *(documented)*
- Structure earns nothing by itself: assessors score the behaviour or
  competency evidenced, and official guidance highlights that the framework's
  job is to set context, demonstrate *your* actions and state outcomes.
  *(documented)*
- Technique has a documented boundary: for UK Civil Service strengths
  questions, candidates are told **not** to rehearse answers, so a pre-built
  STAR story is the wrong tool. *(documented)*
- Evidence does not have to come from paid work — official guidance explicitly
  allows examples from volunteering and life outside work, which matters for
  graduate-scheme and early-careers candidates. *(documented)*
- Choose the structure per question: result-led questions suit CAR's
  compression, failure/learning questions reward STARR/CARL's reflection step,
  initiative questions map naturally onto SOAR's obstacle framing. *(guidance)*
- Interviewers should score content against the published criteria, not
  fluency with the mnemonic; consistent questions and criteria are the
  CIPD/ACAS-recommended basis for fair comparison. *(documented)*

This document answers two questions. For a candidate: *which answer structure
should I use, on whose authority, and how do I fit my experience into it?* For
an interviewer or HR designer: *what does a well-structured answer look like
when I am scoring it, and what does published guidance say about assessing
consistently?* It is the Tier-1 foundation for the Techniques category:
breadth over depth, with deep-dives delegated to planned child documents

Covered: the STAR method and who documents it (UK Civil Service guidance,
National Careers Service, university careers services); documented alternative

title: Answer Frameworks & Techniques: STAR and Beyond

text:
- **Competency-based (behavioural) question** — a question asking for a real
  past example that demonstrates a defined behaviour or competency, as used in
  UK Civil Service behaviours assessment and EPSO's competency-based interview.
- **STAR** — Situation, Task, Action, Result: a structure for evidencing a
  skill through a past example.
- **CAR** — Context, Action, Results: a three-part compression of STAR.
- **STARR / STAR(R)** — STAR plus a final Reflection element on what was
  learned or would be done differently.
- **CARL** — Context, Action, Results, Learning: a reflective four-part
  structure adapted from interview technique.
- **SOAR** — Situation, Obstacle, Action, Result: a practitioner variant that
  replaces Task with Obstacle.
- **Strengths question** — a question about what a candidate enjoys and does
  well, used in UK Civil Service Success Profiles; expressly not to be
  answered with rehearsed examples.
- **Structured interview** — an interview using a predefined set of questions
  asked consistently to all candidates and scored against consistent criteria.
title: Answer Frameworks & Techniques: STAR and Beyond

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

STAR's authority in the UK market is official, not folklore. GOV.UK's Success
Profiles guidance on Civil Service Behaviours states that candidates may be
asked to give examples of when they demonstrated a particular behaviour, and
recommends the STAR method for structuring those examples — allowing the
candidate to set context, demonstrate the actions they took and describe the
outcome [1]. The Civil Service's own recruitment blog repeats the advice at
both stages of the process: "Use the STAR method (Situation, Task, Action,
Result) to structure your responses" for applications, and for interviews,
"Research common Civil Service interview questions for the grade and similar
roles and prepare examples of your work using the STAR method" [3]. The
National Careers Service — the UK government careers advice service — publishes
a dedicated STAR page defining the four elements and recommending the method
for CVs, application forms and interviews [4], and points readers to STAR when
answering common work-history interview questions [5].

Within Success Profiles, the technique's remit is bounded: behaviours are
example-based, but strengths are assessed differently. GOV.UK's strengths
guidance defines strengths as "the things that we do regularly, do well and
that motivate us" and instructs candidates: "Do not rehearse your answers -
because we are looking for your initial response" [2].

On the EU side, EPSO (the European Personnel Selection Office) runs a
competency-based interview "designed to evaluate a number of selected general
competencies, depending on the competition and grade", with candidates
"interviewed for about 10 minutes per competency" and questions "structured to
allow the assessors to evaluate the candidate's selected general competencies"
[6]. The competencies themselves are defined in EPSO's Competency Framework,
which "defines the competencies that are considered essential to perform
effectively in any given job"; the Notice of Competition is "the only official
document that contains detailed information about the actual tests used by
EPSO for a given competition" [7]. The EPSO pages reviewed describe structure
on the assessor's side but do not prescribe a named answer mnemonic to
candidates [6][7].

University careers services — the main advice channel for graduate-scheme
candidates — document the family of structures by name. Oxford's Careers
Service tells candidates to "Use the S.T.A.R technique and make sure to
emphasise your actions if describing a situation" [12]. The University of
Reading documents STAR as "a useful structure for answering application form
and interview questions" and adds a second R for reflection [11]. The
University of Cambridge Careers Service teaches CAR — "context, action,
results" [13]. Arden University documents STAR(R) with an optional Reflection
element [15]. The University of Edinburgh's Reflection Toolkit documents CARL
(Context, Action, Results, Learning), noting "The CARL model is adapted from a
job interview technique" [14]. In the private-sector graduate market, the
employer advice channel targetjobs carries Fidelity International's guidance on
STARR, where reflection is presented as "the difference between a good answer
and a GREAT answer" [16].

| Structure | Elements | Documented by |
|-----------|----------|---------------|
| STAR | Situation, Task, Action, Result | GOV.UK Success Profiles guidance [1]; Civil Service recruitment blog [3]; National Careers Service [4]; Oxford [12]; Reading [11] |
| CAR | Context, Action, Results | University of Cambridge Careers Service [13] |
| STARR / STAR(R) | STAR + Reflection | University of Reading [11]; Arden University [15]; Fidelity International via targetjobs [16] |
| CARL | Context, Action, Results, Learning | University of Edinburgh Reflection Toolkit [14] |
| SOAR | Situation, Obstacle, Action, Result | Practitioner careers writing (named, dated) [17] |
title: Answer Frameworks & Techniques: STAR and Beyond

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

## STAR: the documented elements

The National Careers Service defines the four elements as: "Situation - the
situation you had to deal with; Task - the task you were given to do; Action -
the action you took; Result - what happened as a result of your action and
what you learned" [4]. It recommends the method for showing how you meet the
skills and qualities in a job description, advises keeping examples concise
and conversational rather than sounding over-rehearsed, and states that
examples may be drawn from work, volunteering or personal experience [4]. The
University of Reading's version assigns each element a job: Situation "set[s]
the scene", Task describes what was to be completed and why, Action is "what
**you** did to complete the task" — the element that demonstrates the
competency — and Result "outline[s] the outcome" and its impact [11]. Oxford's
guidance adds the assessor-facing emphasis: emphasise your actions [12].

## CAR: the three-part compression

Cambridge's Careers Service documents CAR as: "**C**ontext: describe the
situation you were in, the task at hand, the problems you faced… **A**ction:
what was your role?… be specific about how you worked and what you did.
**R**esults: what happened? Were you successful?" [13]. Structurally, CAR
folds STAR's Situation and Task into a single Context element; the same
evidence fits both shapes.

## STARR / STAR(R) and CARL: adding reflection

Reading and Arden both document a reflection extension. Arden describes the
extra (R) as optional: the candidate can "reflect on the Situation, Task,
Action and Result" to show "what you have learnt from certain circumstances,
and evidence how you might develop in the future" [15]. Fidelity
International's guidance on targetjobs makes the employer's motive explicit:
"We think that reflecting on your actions and how you might do something
differently next time, can be the difference between a good answer and a GREAT
answer" [16]. Edinburgh's CARL is the same idea from the reflective-practice
side: Context ("Briefly describe the context of you[r] experience"), Action
("Explain what actions you took"), Results ("Explain what happened as a result
of your actions"), Learning ("Identify what you have learned") — and the
toolkit records that the model is adapted from a job interview technique [14].
Note that the National Careers Service's own definition of the STAR Result
element already includes "what you learned" [4], so the reflective variants
formalise something the baseline guidance gestures at.

## SOAR: the practitioner variant

SOAR (Situation, Obstacle, Action, Result) is documented in named, dated
practitioner careers writing as "a structured way to answer behavioral
interview questions", replacing STAR's Task with Obstacle to emphasise
problem-solving [17]. No official UK or EU guidance reviewed for this document
names SOAR; it is recorded here as practitioner practice, not as an
officially recommended technique [17].

## Selecting evidence

The documented guidance on evidence selection is consistent across sources: the
job description or advert lists the skills, qualities or behaviours sought, and
the example is chosen to show how the candidate meets them [4][1]. Candidates
without much work history are explicitly permitted to use examples from outside
paid work — volunteering and personal experience in the National Careers
Service guidance [4] — and the Civil Service blog advises identifying the key
behaviours required for the target role and preparing specific examples that
demonstrate them [3]. For strengths questions the documented preparation is
different in kind: reflect on personal strengths and preferred ways of
working, but do not rehearse, because assessors want the initial response [2].
title: Answer Frameworks & Techniques: STAR and Beyond

text:
Each entry carries a provenance label per the house rules. The one-line note
after each question is **coaching** — it says which structure fits and why,
pointing back to the evidence layer; it is not part of the sourced question.

## Published questions from public careers guidance

1. When have you faced a challenging situation? *(sourced: National Careers Service)* [5] — Note: classic full-STAR territory; the challenge is the Situation, and the marks live in the Action [4][12].
2. Can you tell us about a personal achievement at work? *(sourced: National Careers Service)* [5] — Note: suits CAR's compression [13]: brief context, then lead with actions and a concrete, measurable Result.
3. Have you ever taken the initiative? *(sourced: National Careers Service)* [5] — Note: maps naturally onto SOAR's obstacle/objective framing [17] — what you chose to tackle unprompted, then Action and Result.
4. Have you ever failed at a task? *(sourced: National Careers Service)* [5] — Note: a reflection question in disguise; STARR/CARL fit best because the Learning step is the actual answer [14][15][16].
5. What are your main strengths? *(sourced: National Careers Service)* [5] — Note: not a story question — do not force STAR; UK Civil Service strengths guidance says unrehearsed, authentic responses are wanted [2].

## Composed — in the style of UK Civil Service behaviours [1]

1. Tell us about a time you had to deliver a piece of work to a tight deadline while quality concerns were being raised. What did you do? *(composed)* — Note: STAR with a deliberately fat Action section; the tension between pace and quality is resolved in what *you* did [1][12].
2. Describe a situation where you used incomplete or conflicting evidence to reach a decision that others questioned. *(composed)* — Note: STAR with a strong Task element — name the decision that was yours to own before narrating actions [1].
3. Give an example of working with people from very different backgrounds or professional areas to deliver a shared outcome. *(composed)* — Note: CAR works well [13]; the interpersonal actions are the evidence, so compress context hard.

## Composed — in the style of the EPSO competency-based interview [6]

1. Describe an occasion when you had to reprioritise your work at short notice. How did you decide what mattered most? *(composed)* — Note: use a compressed STAR; EPSO allows roughly 10 minutes per competency including follow-ups, so the example must land quickly [6].
2. Tell us about a time you explained a complex or technical issue to a non-specialist audience. *(composed)* — Note: STAR with the Action expanded into steps (how you adapted the message), since the questions are structured for assessors to evaluate the competency [6].

## Composed — private-sector and graduate-scheme style

1. Tell me about a time something you were responsible for went wrong. Looking back, what would you do differently? *(composed)* — Note: the question explicitly invites the second R — answer in STARR/CARL, ending on learning applied since [14][16].
2. Describe a project or activity you started on your own initiative. What obstacles did you hit, and what came of it? *(composed)* — Note: SOAR-shaped by construction [17]; naming the obstacle distinguishes initiative from assigned work.
3. Give an example from university, work or life outside work where you kept a team on track when motivation dropped. *(composed)* — Note: STAR with non-work evidence — official guidance expressly allows volunteering and personal examples [4].
title: Answer Frameworks & Techniques: STAR and Beyond

text:
**Coaching layer — this is guidance, not documented fact.** It points back to
the evidence layer throughout.

**Pick the structure per question, not per interview.** All five documented
structures carry the same payload — context, your actions, the outcome — so
the choice is about emphasis. As a working rule: use STAR as the default (it
is the one named in official UK guidance [1][3][4]); collapse to CAR when the
question is achievement- or result-led and context is obvious [13]; extend to
STARR/CARL whenever the question contains "went wrong", "failed", "learned"
or "differently" [14][15][16]; borrow SOAR's obstacle framing for initiative
questions [17]. Never bring a story structure to a strengths question — the
documented instruction is not to rehearse [2].

**Weight the answer towards Action.** Oxford's documented advice — emphasise
your actions [12] — generalises: Situation and Task/Context exist only to make
the Action legible, and the Result exists to prove the Action worked. A
serviceable skeleton for the first sourced question above ("When have you
faced a challenging situation?" [5]): one or two sentences of situation
("In my placement year, our two-person support rota lost its senior member the
week a major client onboarded"); one sentence of task ("I was left owning the
onboarding calls"); four to six sentences of first-person action, each
starting with a verb; one or two sentences of result with a number or a named
consequence, plus — if the question invites it — one sentence of reflection.

**Speak as "I", evidence as "we".** Assessors are evaluating the behaviour
*you* demonstrated [1]; acknowledge the team in the Situation, then narrate
your own decisions and actions.

**Build the story once, flex it many ways.** Because CAR, STAR, STARR and CARL
are nested, one well-chosen example can be compressed or extended live to fit
the question and the time available — useful in EPSO-style interviews where
time per competency is limited [6]. Preparing six to eight such examples,
mapped against the behaviours or competencies in the advert [3][1], is the
core of interview preparation; the planned building-evidence-bank document
will treat this in depth.

**For interviewers**: a well-structured answer is not a better answer, it is a
more *scoreable* one — the structure surfaces the actions and outcomes your
criteria ask about [1]. If a candidate's answer lacks structure, a neutral
probe ("what did you do next?", "what was the outcome?") retrieves the same
information; consistency of questioning across candidates is the documented
fairness mechanism [9][10].
title: Answer Frameworks & Techniques: STAR and Beyond

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

Behaviours in the UK Civil Service are assessed through multiple methods —
application-form examples, tests, interviews, assessment-centre exercises and
simulations — against a published set of nine behaviours (Seeing the Big
Picture, Changing and Improving, Making Effective Decisions, Leadership,
Communicating and Influencing, Working Together, Developing Self and Others,
Managing a Quality Service, Delivering at Pace), with recruiting managers
choosing the behaviours relevant to the role and level [1]. The same guidance
frames STAR from the assessor's side: it lets the candidate set context, show
the actions taken and describe outcomes — i.e. it produces the material the
behaviour is scored on [1]. Strengths, by contrast, are assessed through
interviews, recorded video interviews, situational strengths tests, simulation
assessments or personality tests, with no right or wrong answers and an
explicit instruction against rehearsal [2].

In EPSO competitions, the competency-based interview evaluates selected
general competencies specified per competition and grade, at about 10 minutes
per competency, with questions structured so that assessors can evaluate those
competencies [6]; the competencies are defined in EPSO's Competency Framework
and the Notice of Competition is the authoritative statement of what a given
competition tests [7].

Professional-body guidance converges on structure as the fairness mechanism.
CIPD's selection guidance recommends structured interviews — "asking a
predefined set of questions, in the same order, to all candidates", scored
against consistent criteria — because this makes it "easier for the interview
panels to make direct and fair comparisons between candidates, using objective
criteria"; it further recommends panel interviews over single interviewers and
that interviewers "assign scores to each candidate response independently
before discussing them as a group" [9]. ACAS advises employers to ask each
applicant "the same questions where possible" to ensure equal treatment, and
to check that the final decision is "based on how each applicant met each
point in the job description and person specification" [10]. Read together
with the candidate-side guidance, the documented picture is symmetrical: the
candidate's structure exists to feed the assessor's criteria, and the
assessor's consistency exists to make comparisons of that evidence fair
[1][9][10].
title: Answer Frameworks & Techniques: STAR and Beyond

text:
**Coaching layer — labelled guidance,** each item tied back to the documented
criteria it violates.

- **Front-loading context.** Spending half the answer on Situation starves the
  element that is actually scored — the documented advice is to emphasise your
  actions [12][1].
- **"We" throughout.** An answer narrated entirely as team achievement leaves
  no individual behaviour to score; the guidance asks what *you* did [1][4].
- **No result.** Arden's guidance notes the Result is often overlooked —
  "Without this part, the interviewer will not know what the outcome was"
  [15]. An answer without an outcome is an anecdote, not evidence.
- **Forcing STAR onto strengths questions.** The UK guidance explicitly wants
  unrehearsed initial responses to strengths questions [2]; a polished story
  answers the wrong question.
- **Sounding scripted.** The National Careers Service advises keeping examples
  conversational to avoid sounding over-rehearsed [4]; recite the structure,
  not a script.
- **One mega-example.** Reusing a single example for every question narrows
  the behaviours evidenced; official advice is to prepare examples against the
  key behaviours required for the role [3].
- **(Interviewers) crediting polish over content.** The documented basis for
  decisions is how the applicant met the job description and person
  specification [10], scored consistently [9] — not fluency with a mnemonic.
- **(Interviewers) inconsistent probing.** Rescuing one candidate's
  unstructured answer with follow-ups but not another's undermines the
  same-questions consistency ACAS and CIPD recommend [10][9].
title: Answer Frameworks & Techniques: STAR and Beyond

text:
**Coaching layer.** Drills a reader can run this week.

1. **Build a starter evidence bank.** Take a live job advert; list its named
   behaviours or competencies [1][3]. Against each, note one real example —
   work, study, volunteering or outside life all count [4]. Aim for six to
   eight distinct examples covering the list.
2. **Write one STAR answer, then compress and extend it.** Draft an answer to
   the challenging-situation question (Question Bank, first sourced entry) in
   full STAR [4]; rewrite it as CAR in half the length [13]; then
   add a Reflection sentence to make it STARR [15][16]. Notice what each shape
   foregrounds.
3. **Timed delivery.** Answer two Question Bank items aloud in about two
   minutes each, keeping most of the time in Action. For EPSO-style practice,
   remember the whole competency slot is around 10 minutes including
   follow-ups [6].
4. **Self-score against published criteria.** Record yourself, then score the
   answer only on evidence of the target behaviour as defined in the published
   framework [1] — not on delivery. If you cannot find the behaviour in your
   own transcript, the assessor cannot either.
5. **Mock panel (pairs).** One person asks three Question Bank items
   consistently, probes neutrally ("what did you do next?"), takes notes, and
   scores independently before discussing — mirroring the CIPD-recommended
   discipline [9].
6. **Strengths contrast drill.** Have a partner ask the strengths question
   from the Question Bank ("What are your main strengths?") cold and answer
   without preparation, then reflect on your preferred ways of working as the
   guidance suggests [2] — feel the difference from example-based answering.
title: Answer Frameworks & Techniques: STAR and Beyond

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.** Signposts only; siblings treat these
in depth.

- **Reasonable adjustments.** Under the Equality Act 2010, section 20, there
  is a duty to make adjustments where a disabled person would otherwise be at
  a substantial disadvantage — including adjusting provisions, criteria or
  practices and providing auxiliary aids [8]. ACAS guidance applies this to
  recruitment: employers must make reasonable adjustments for disabled
  applicants and interviewees, for example ensuring the venue is accessible or
  providing a sign language interpreter [10]. A rigidly timed, orally
  delivered STAR interview is a "practice" that may need adjusting.
- **Consistency as fairness.** ACAS advises asking each applicant the same
  questions where possible to ensure equal treatment, and basing decisions on
  how each applicant met the job description and person specification [10];
  CIPD frames the same discipline as the basis for fair comparison [9].
- **Candidate data.** ACAS notes that interviews may only be recorded with the
  person's permission and that interview notes must be stored securely and
  confidentially in line with data protection law [10].
title: Answer Frameworks & Techniques: STAR and Beyond

  change without version history. All URLs were opened and verified on
  2026-07-19; the mnemonics' exact wording varies between sources.
- Variant names are not standardised. STARR, STAR(R), STAR-L and similar
  labels circulate in careers materials with overlapping meanings; this
  document records only the variants it could verify to a named publisher.
  Treat any other label as unverified until documented.
- SOAR rests on practitioner sources only; it should not be presented to
  candidates as an officially recommended technique.
- Employer practice varies. This document records published guidance;
  individual employers (including departments and EU institutions) may issue
  their own candidate instructions, which take precedence for that process.
- EPSO's selection model has been undergoing reform; the Notice of Competition
  for a specific competition is the only authoritative statement of its tests
  [7], and details here may lag current competitions.

  planned star-technique deep-dive.

text:
This is the foundation document for the UK Frameworks category. It surveys the
main published frameworks that shape competency- and values-based interviews in
UK recruitment: the Civil Service **Success Profiles** framework and its nine
**Behaviours** (the anchor for the whole category, because it is the most
completely documented) [1] [2]; **NHS values-based recruitment**, built on the
six values of the NHS Constitution [4] [6]; the College of Policing
**Competency and Values Framework (CVF)**, refreshed in 2024 around the Code of
Ethics [8]; and the less standardised world of **private-sector and
graduate-scheme employers**, where survey data shows a competency lens still
underpins most early-careers selection [16].

The headline pattern across all four segments is the same: a published (or at
least internally defined) set of behaviours or values, assessed through
structured interviews in which every candidate faces comparable questions
scored against pre-agreed criteria [12] [14]. What differs is the object of
assessment (behaviours, values, strengths or a mix), the vocabulary, and where
the interview sits in the wider selection funnel.

Confidence is **Medium**: the public-sector sections rest on official framework
documents (GOV.UK, HEE/NHS, College of Policing, legislation), which would
support High on their own, but the private-sector and graduate-scheme landscape
rests substantially on careers-service reporting of member-only survey data and
employer careers pages, which involves more synthesis.
title: UK Competency Frameworks: The Landscape

text:
- The Civil Service recruits with Success Profiles: five elements —
  Behaviours, Strengths, Ability, Experience, Technical — selected per vacancy;
  no role uses every element automatically. *(documented)* [1]
- There are nine Civil Service Behaviours, each defined at six grade bands from
  AA/AO up to Director/Director General; job adverts name the behaviours being
  tested. *(documented)* [2] [3]
- NHS values-based recruitment requires (for HEE-funded training programmes)
  an individual, face-to-face structured interview that assesses candidates
  against the values of the NHS Constitution; NHS employers are encouraged to
  do the same. *(documented)* [4] [6]
- The College of Policing CVF (2024) defines six competencies at three levels,
  underpinned by three values drawn from the Code of Ethics; it is the basis of
  national police recruitment, promotion and development processes.
  *(documented)* [8]
- Among graduate employers surveyed by the ISE, a competency-based lens still
  underpinned most selection strategies in 2022 (68%), with strengths-based
  (56%) and values-based (35%) approaches increasingly blended in.
  *(documented)* [16]
- Across all frameworks, the interview itself is structured: same or similar
  questions per candidate, scored against pre-agreed criteria — this is the
  common professional-practice core, whatever the branding. *(documented)*
  [12] [14]
- For candidates the practical move is the same everywhere: find the published
  framework for your target employer, and prepare evidence stories mapped to
  its named behaviours or values. *(guidance)*
- Interviewers designing UK processes should treat these public frameworks as
  worked examples of good practice: published criteria, levelled descriptors,
  and structured scoring. *(guidance)*

text:
Covered: the UK landscape of competency/behaviour/values frameworks used in
recruitment interviews — Civil Service Success Profiles and Behaviours; NHS
values-based recruitment; the College of Policing CVF; and how private-sector
and graduate-scheme employers structure competency interviews, using published
survey data and one named employer example (Unilever) with public
documentation. For each, it explains where the competency interview sits in the
hiring process.

Not covered (deliberately — this is a Tier-1 breadth document): grade-by-grade
behaviour descriptors, full question banks per framework, EU institutions
(see `eu-frameworks-foundation`), interview answering techniques in depth
(see `techniques-foundation`), and format mechanics such as video interviews
and assessment centres (see `formats-foundation`). Scotland/Wales/Northern
Ireland public-sector variants are out of scope for this edition.
