text:
Prefer the shared `GLOSSARY.md`; the following are used in this document's
narrowed sense.

- **Success Profile** — the Civil Service recruitment framework; a per-vacancy
  bundle of up to five assessed elements (Behaviours, Strengths, Ability,
  Experience, Technical) [1].
- **Behaviour (Civil Service sense)** — "actions and activities people do
  which result in effective performance", defined at grade-specific levels [3].
- **Values-based recruitment (VBR)** — recruiting "on the basis that their
  individual values and behaviours align with the values of the NHS
  Constitution" [5].
- **CVF** — the College of Policing Competency and Values Framework: six
  competencies at three levels plus core values, used across police HR
  processes [8].
- **Structured interview** — an interview asking a standardised set of
  predetermined questions, scored against predetermined criteria applied to
  all candidates [6] [14].
title: UK Competency Frameworks: The Landscape

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

The four segments of the UK landscape compare as follows.

| Segment | Framework | Assessed constructs | Levels | Public documentation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Civil Service | Success Profiles [1] | 9 Behaviours + Strengths, Ability, Experience, Technical [1] [2] | 6 grade bands (AA/AO → Director/DG) for Behaviours [2] | Full framework on GOV.UK [1] |
| NHS | Values-based recruitment [5] | 6 NHS Constitution values [4] | Not levelled; same values for all staff [4] | HEE framework + NHS Employers tools [6] [7] |
| Policing | Competency and Values Framework (2024) [8] | 6 competencies + 3 values from the Code of Ethics [8] | 3 levels (practitioner → senior/executive), competencies only [8] | College of Policing framework and guidance [8] [9] |
| Graduate schemes / private sector | Employer-specific | Competencies, strengths and values, typically blended [16] | Employer-specific | Varies; e.g. Unilever publishes its selection stages [18] |

Three context points frame everything below.

First, the Civil Service is the anchor reference: Success Profiles is the
recruitment framework "used within the Civil Service", published in full on
GOV.UK and most recently updated in January 2025 [1] [2]. Because its
behaviours, levels and element definitions are public, it is the most common
vocabulary in UK public-sector interviewing and the best-documented model for
private study.

Second, the public-sector frameworks have been converging on values alongside
competencies: NHS recruitment is explicitly values-based [5], and the 2024 CVF
update rebuilt the police values around the principles of the new Code of
Ethics [8]. The College's guidance describes the CVF interview approach as
continuous with "previous competency-based or values-based interviews" — past
behaviour with probing, plus occasional hypothetical scenarios [8].

Third, in the graduate market the ISE — which describes its annual Student
Recruitment Survey as covering "the largest group of early careers employers
in the UK" [15] — found in 2022 that 68% of employers reported a
competency-based lens underpinning their recruitment strategies, 56% used a
strengths-based perspective and 35% incorporated values-based exercises, as
reported by Prospects' Luminate research site [16]. The three lenses are
complements in practice, not rivals.
title: UK Competency Frameworks: The Landscape

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

## Civil Service Success Profiles: the five elements

Success Profiles replace a one-size-fits-all competency model with five
elements a vacancy can draw on: **Behaviours** (actions resulting in effective
job performance), **Strengths** (things done regularly, done well and
motivating), **Ability** (aptitude or potential to meet the required
standard), **Experience** (knowledge or mastery gained through involvement or
exposure), and **Technical** (specific professional skills, knowledge or
qualifications) [1]. Not every element is used for every role; recruiting
managers select the mix per vacancy, and the job advert states which elements
are assessed [1] [3].

## The nine Civil Service Behaviours and their levels

The nine Behaviours, with their official one-line summaries, are [2]:

1. **Seeing the big picture** — understand how your role fits with and
   supports organisational objectives.
2. **Changing and improving** — seek out opportunities to create effective
   change and suggest innovative ideas.
3. **Making effective decisions** — use evidence and knowledge to support
   accurate, expert decisions and advice.
4. **Leadership** — show pride and passion for public service; create and
   engage others in delivering a shared vision.
5. **Communicating and influencing** — communicate purpose and direction with
   clarity, integrity and enthusiasm.
6. **Working together** — form effective partnerships and relationships with
   people both internally and externally.
7. **Developing self and others** — focus on continuous learning and
   development for self, others and the organisation.
8. **Managing a quality service** — deliver service objectives with
   professional excellence, expertise and efficiency.
9. **Delivering at pace** — take responsibility for delivering timely and
   quality results with focus and drive.

Each behaviour carries example statements at six grade bands: AA/AO, EO,
HEO/SEO, Grade 7/Grade 6, Deputy Director, and Director/Director General [2].
Candidates are not asked to demonstrate all nine for one role; the recruiting
manager chooses the behaviours best suited to the job [3].

**Where the interview sits.** A Civil Service campaign may combine a CV and
personal statement sift (with behaviour evidence), online ability tests,
presentations, an interview (face-to-face or video, often before a panel) and
sometimes a full assessment centre; behaviours can be assessed on the
application form, at interview through example-based or situational questions,
or in real-time structured behavioural assessments [3]. GOV.UK notes that
behaviours may be assessed through interviews using situational questions [2].

## NHS values-based recruitment

The NHS Constitution for England sets six values: working together for
patients; respect and dignity; commitment to quality of care; compassion;
improving lives; and everyone counts [4]. Values-based recruitment (VBR) is
the approach of recruiting students, trainees and employees "on the basis that
their individual values and behaviours align with the values of the NHS
Constitution"; the national VBR framework was published in October 2014 and
refreshed in April 2016 by Health Education England (HEE), and from 1 April
2015 all higher-education institutions contracted by HEE to deliver NHS-funded
training programmes must assess students for NHS Constitution values [5] [6].

The framework's evidence review identifies the methods shown to be effective
for recruiting for values: structured interviews, multiple mini interviews
(MMIs), selection centres, and situational judgement tests for screening [6].
Its national core requirement CR4 mandates "an individual, face-to-face
structured interview to include an assessment of values", which may take place
as an MMI, within a selection centre, or in conjunction with SJT screening
[6]. The framework defines a values-based interview as a structured interview
focused on the candidate's values, distinguished by probing questions designed
to reveal what the candidate considers important [6]. NHS Employers describes
VBR delivery for employing organisations through pre-screening assessments,
values-based interviewing techniques (including role play and written
responses to scenarios) and assessment-centre approaches, supported by a
behaviour framework usable as interview scoring criteria [7].

**Where the interview sits.** For NHS-funded student recruitment the values
interview is a required stage (CR4); for NHS employers it typically sits after
screening (which may include SJTs) and alongside or within selection-centre
exercises [6] [7].

## College of Policing Competency and Values Framework

The CVF was introduced in 2016 and updated in 2024 to incorporate the
principles underpinning the new Code of Ethics [8]. The 2024 CVF consists of
six competencies, each at three levels, underpinned by three core values [8]:

- **Competencies:** We are emotionally aware; We take ownership; We
  collaborate; We support and inspire; We analyse critically; We are
  innovative and open-minded [8].
- **Values:** Respect and empathy; Courage; Public service — reflecting the
  Code of Ethics principles; values are not levelled and apply to all roles
  [8].
- **Levels:** level 1 broadly maps to practitioners, level 2 to
  supervisors/managers, level 3 to senior managers/executives; levels reflect
  increasing behavioural complexity and apply to competencies only [8].

The 2016 original differed in two structural ways: its six competencies were
grouped into three clusters (resolute, compassionate and committed; inclusive,
enabling and visionary leadership; intelligent, creative and informed
policing), and it was underpinned by four values — integrity, impartiality,
public service and transparency; its levels were explicitly cumulative, higher
levels building on the behaviours of preceding ones [9]. The 2024 guidance
removed the clusters for simplicity and notes that forces in transition may
still use the 2016 version, so candidates attending College assessment
processes should check which version they are being assessed against [8].

**Where the interview sits.** The CVF is the basis of national HR processes
including national assessments for recruitment and promotion, professional
development review, and leadership development [8]. For recruitment and
selection interviews, the College's guidance states it is good practice to ask
about examples of past behaviour and probe for evidence, sometimes adding
hypothetical future scenarios, with most of the interview focused on past
behaviour; assessment-centre exercises should measure a small number of named
competencies and candidates should be told which [8].

## Graduate schemes and the private sector

There is no single private-sector framework; each employer defines its own
competencies or values, and public documentation varies. The evidence base for
the overall pattern is the ISE's annual Student Recruitment Survey [15], whose
2022 findings — as publicly reported by Luminate (Prospects) — were that 68%
of employers reported a competency-based lens underpinning recruitment
strategy; 56% used a strengths-based perspective; 35% incorporated
values-based exercises; around one in ten had added AI to parts of selection
(most often candidate pre-screening and video-interview analysis); and
personality-test use had fallen from roughly a third of employers in 2014 to
9% in 2022 [16].

Prospects' guidance for graduate candidates describes the standard shape of
the competency interview itself: structured questions each targeting a
specific skill, answers compared against pre-determined criteria, typically
three to six competency questions in a 45–60 minute interview, particularly
favoured by large graduate recruiters and often embedded in assessment centres
[17].

A documented named example: **Unilever's Future Leaders Programme (UK &
Ireland)** publishes a four-stage selection process — online application;
profile assessment (a personality "portrait" plus an online assessment of
cognitive, emotional and social traits); a digital interview of three short
questions followed by a business case solving "real-world problems using
Unilever scenarios"; and a Discovery Centre day including project meetings,
business challenges, a team meeting and an interview [18]. TARGETjobs' guide
to the same programme describes the digital-interview questions as
hypothetical and advises candidates to prepare by studying Unilever's culture,
values and Standards of Leadership [19]. This illustrates the wider
graduate-scheme pattern: a multi-stage funnel in which the interview arrives
late, after online assessment, and blends hypothetical/situational material
with employer-specific values.
title: UK Competency Frameworks: The Landscape

text:
Fifteen questions illustrating the *style* of each framework. Provenance per
the house rule: *(sourced …)* questions are published by the named source;
*(composed)* questions were written for this knowledge base in the style of
the cited framework and are **not** real or leaked employer questions.

## Civil Service Success Profiles style (Behaviours and Strengths)

1. Tell us about a time when you had to use evidence from several sources to make a decision under time pressure. What did you conclude and why? *(composed — in the style of the Behaviour "Making effective decisions" [2])*
2. Describe a time when you built a working relationship with another team or organisation to deliver something neither of you could deliver alone. *(composed — in the style of the Behaviour "Working together" [2])*
3. Give an example of a time you had to deliver a piece of work to a tight deadline while maintaining quality. How did you organise yourself and others? *(composed — in the style of the Behaviour "Delivering at pace" [2])*
4. Tell us about a time you spotted how a change in your organisation's wider priorities affected your own work, and what you did about it. *(composed — in the style of the Behaviour "Seeing the big picture" [2])*
5. What kind of task do you find yourself volunteering for, and what does that tell us about you? *(composed — a strengths-style prompt of the kind Success Profiles pairs with behaviour questions [1] [3])*

## NHS values-based style

1. Describe a time when you noticed that someone — a patient, customer or colleague — was distressed. What did you do, and what were you thinking about as you acted? *(composed — in the style of a values-based interview probing "compassion" [4] [6])*
2. Tell us about a time you worked with people from other teams or professions to improve the experience of a service user. What was your contribution? *(composed — in the style of "working together for patients" [4] [6])*
3. Give an example of a time you saw practice that fell short of the standard you expect. What did you do, and why did it matter to you? *(composed — a values probe in the style of "commitment to quality of care", with follow-up designed to reveal what the candidate considers important [6])*

## College of Policing CVF style

1. Tell us about a time you made a mistake or missed something important. What did you do once you realised, and what changed afterwards? *(composed — in the style of the CVF competency "We take ownership" [8])*
2. Describe a situation where you had to weigh up incomplete or conflicting information before acting. How did you test your thinking? *(composed — in the style of the CVF competency "We analyse critically" [8])*
3. Give an example of a time you did the right thing even though it was uncomfortable or unpopular. What made it difficult? *(composed — a values probe in the style of the CVF value "Courage" [8])*

## Graduate-scheme / general competency style

1. Describe a situation in which you led a team. *(sourced: Prospects, "Competency-based interviews", June 2025)* [17]
2. Give an example of a time you handled conflict in the workplace. *(sourced: Prospects, "Competency-based interviews", June 2025)* [17]
3. Tell me about a time when your communication skills improved a situation. *(sourced: Prospects, "Competency-based interviews", June 2025)* [17]
4. Tell me about a time when you showed integrity and professionalism. *(sourced: Prospects, "Competency-based interviews", June 2025)* [17]
title: UK Competency Frameworks: The Landscape

text:
**Coaching layer — this is guidance, not documented fact.**

- **Map before you draft.** Identify which framework governs your interview:
  the job advert names it in the Civil Service (elements and behaviours are
  listed per vacancy [3]), NHS adverts and person specifications reference the
  Constitution values [6], and police roles are assessed against named CVF
  competencies [8]. Prepare one evidence story per named behaviour/value, not
  a generic set.
- **Use a structured narrative.** Prospects recommends STARR — Situation,
  Task, Action, Result, Reflection — for competency answers [17]; the
  techniques deep-dive (`techniques-foundation`) covers STAR variants in
  detail. Whatever the mnemonic, the load-bearing parts are the *action* (what
  **you** personally did) and the *result*.
- **Match the level, not just the behaviour.** Civil Service behaviours are
  defined differently at each grade band [2]; CVF competencies at each of
  three levels [8]. A Grade 7 answer about "Leadership" should show shaping
  and engaging others, not just personal diligence. Read the descriptor for
  your target level and select evidence at that altitude.
- **Expect values probes to go one layer deeper.** NHS-style values
  interviewing is defined by probing follow-ups that reveal what you consider
  important [6]. Prepare not just what you did but *why* — the reasoning shows
  the value.
- **In strengths portions, answer promptly and honestly.** Civil Service
  guidance to candidates is to give your genuine first response to strengths
  questions, in contrast to the prepared, structured examples expected for
  behaviours [3].
title: UK Competency Frameworks: The Landscape

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

- The common documented core is the structured interview: CIPD's selection
  factsheet describes asking all candidates a predefined set of questions in
  the same order and scoring responses against pre-agreed criteria, preferably
  before a panel rather than a sole interviewer, to make responses comparable
  and reduce bias [14]. Acas similarly advises asking each applicant the same
  questions where possible and basing decisions on how each applicant met the
  job description and person specification [12].
- In the Civil Service, the recruiting manager selects which elements and
  which behaviours a vacancy assesses, and the interview may combine
  behaviour, strength, experience and technical questions; panels (e.g. of
  three) are used at interview [1] [3].
- In the NHS model, the interview must be structured and individual, with
  values assessed explicitly; NHS Employers provides a behaviour framework
  that can be used directly as interview scoring criteria, with examples of
  behaviours that do and do not align with the Constitution values [6] [7].
- College of Policing guidance tells assessors that the CVF's example
  behavioural indicators are illustrative, not exhaustive — the framework
  "should not be used as a simple checklist" in selection, and assessors
  should stay open to diverse ways of demonstrating a competency that fit its
  definition [8]. Interviews should focus mostly on past behaviour with
  probing for evidence; assessment exercises should measure a small number of
  competencies, with candidates told which, and assessors trained on what
  evidence to look for [8].
- Graduate recruiters increasingly automate the early funnel — around one in
  ten ISE-surveyed employers used AI, most often for pre-screening and
  video-interview analysis — which repositions the human competency interview
  as a later, higher-stakes stage [16].
title: UK Competency Frameworks: The Landscape

text:
**Coaching layer — this is guidance, not documented fact.**

- **Preparing for the wrong framework.** Bringing Civil-Service-style
  behaviour stories to an NHS values interview misses the point of the
  values probe (the *why*, not just the *what*) [6]. Check the advert's named
  framework first.
- **Ignoring the level descriptors.** Answering a Grade 6 "Leadership"
  question with EO-level evidence under-scores against the published
  grade-band examples [2]; the same applies to CVF levels [8].
- **Treating the framework as a checklist — in either chair.** The College of
  Policing explicitly warns assessors against checklist use of the CVF [8];
  candidates who mechanically name-drop indicator phrases produce answers
  that fit the words but not the definition.
- **Hypothetical answers to past-behaviour questions.** Prospects flags
  "I would usually…" responses as a standard mistake; specific, real events
  with outcomes are wanted [17], and CVF guidance likewise centres the
  interview on past behaviour [8].
- **For interviewers: unstructured drift.** Departing from the planned
  questions and criteria undermines the comparability that is the documented
  rationale of the structured interview [12] [14] and invites the fairness
  risks in the next-but-one section.
- **Assuming the framework version.** Police forces could still be using the
  2016 CVF during transition [8]; candidates and panel members should confirm
  which version applies before preparing or scoring.
title: UK Competency Frameworks: The Landscape

text:
**Coaching layer.**

1. **Framework mapping drill (30 min).** Take one live vacancy from Civil
   Service Jobs, one NHS vacancy and one graduate scheme. For each, list the
   named behaviours/values in the advert and match one of your own evidence
   stories to each. Note the gaps — those are your preparation targets.
2. **Level-lift drill (45 min).** Pick one Civil Service Behaviour and read
   its example statements at two adjacent grade bands on GOV.UK [2]. Write a
   STARR answer at the lower band, then rewrite it to evidence the higher
   band. Identify exactly what changed (scope, others involved, time horizon).
3. **Values probe rehearsal (paired, 20 min).** Answer question 6 or 8 from
   the Question Bank while a partner asks only "why?" and "what mattered to
   you there?" follow-ups, in the spirit of the VBI probing style [6].
   Debrief: did your answers reveal values or just actions?
4. **Assessor's seat (30 min).** Using the nine behaviour summaries [2] as
   criteria, score a friend's recorded answer to question 1 on a simple
   1–5 scale with written justification. Notice how much easier scoring is
   when the question targeted a single named behaviour — that is the design
   logic of every framework in this document.
5. **Timed set (25 min).** Answer questions 12–15 aloud, one take each,
   3–4 minutes per answer — the density implied by three to six competency
   questions in a 45–60 minute interview [17].
title: UK Competency Frameworks: The Landscape

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

- Employers must follow discrimination law when advertising roles,
  interviewing and deciding whom to appoint; direct and indirect
  discrimination in recruitment are both unlawful under the Equality Act 2010
  [13].
- Under section 20 of the Equality Act 2010, employers owe a duty to make
  reasonable adjustments for disabled people; Acas notes this applies to job
  applicants and interviewees at any stage of recruitment [10] [12]. The
  frameworks operationalise this: Civil Service campaigns offer adjustments
  for any element of the assessment process [3], and CVF guidance tells
  candidates they can request reasonable adjustments to assessments, including
  for neurodiversity [8].
- Section 60 of the Equality Act 2010 restricts asking about a candidate's
  health or disability before a job offer, subject to limited exceptions
  (e.g. establishing the need for adjustments to the assessment itself) [11].
  Interview questions in any framework must be designed with this boundary in
  mind.
- Structured, criteria-based interviewing is itself presented by professional
  bodies as a fairness mechanism: consistent questions and objective scoring
  make it less likely that decisions rest on personal bias [12] [14].
title: UK Competency Frameworks: The Landscape

text:
- **Frameworks move.** The Behaviours page was updated as recently as January
  2025 [2], and the CVF was substantially revised in 2024 with a transition
  period in which both versions circulate [8]. Any deep-dive or coaching built
  on this document must re-verify the current version at time of use.
- **The HEE VBR framework is dated March 2016** and HEE has since been merged
  into NHS England; the framework pages remain live [5] [6], but local trust
  practice may have evolved beyond the national core requirements. Treat
  trust-level detail as unverified until the NHS deep-dive documents it.
- **Private-sector claims are the weakest layer.** The ISE survey itself is
  member-only [15]; the figures here come from Prospects' Luminate reporting
  of it [16] and describe 2022 — AI-in-selection numbers in particular are
  likely to have shifted. Employer processes (e.g. Unilever's stages [18])
  change without notice.
- **Composed questions are illustrative.** They mimic the published style of
  each framework but have no official status; do not present them to
  candidates as real past questions from any employer.
- **This is a breadth document.** Level descriptors, scoring scales and full
  question banks are deliberately summarised; relying on this document alone
  to prepare for a specific Civil Service, NHS or police campaign would
  under-prepare you — use the deep-dives when published.
title: UK Competency Frameworks: The Landscape

title: EU Competency Frameworks: EPSO & the EU Institutions

text:
This is the foundation document for the EU Frameworks category. It surveys how
competencies are defined and assessed for careers in the European Union
institutions — where the European Personnel Selection Office (EPSO) runs the
selection procedures — and, briefly, how competency-based interviewing is used
by private-sector employers across the EU market.

The single most important fact for any reader is that EPSO's selection model
changed fundamentally in 2023. The old model, in place since 2010, tested eight
general competencies at an assessment centre that included a competency-based
interview. Under the new model, decided by EPSO's Management Board on
31 January 2023, the assessment centre and its oral tests "cease to exist":
competitions now run on remotely proctored written tests (reasoning, EU
knowledge or field-specific MCQ, a written/case-study test, and in some
competitions a digital-skills test), and candidates' general competencies are
instead reviewed via **recruitment interviews conducted by the institutions
after EPSO publishes the reserve list** [3]. A new eight-competency framework
(2022) — from "Critical thinking, analysing & creative problem-solving" to
"Intrapreneurship" — remains the reference standard for what EU officials are
expected to demonstrate [1] [2].

The document is rated **Medium** confidence: the core claims (the competency
framework, the new competition model, contract-agent selection, reserve-list
recruitment) rest on official EPSO/EU Careers and EUR-Lex documents, but the
description of how recruitment-stage interviews run in practice involves some
synthesis from practitioner sources, and EPSO's process is still visibly in
flux (its first big post-reform generalist competition ran in early 2026).
title: EU Competency Frameworks: EPSO & the EU Institutions

text:
- EPSO is the interinstitutional office that selects staff for the EU
  institutions; permanent officials are recruited through open competitions
  that end in a **reserve list**, from which institutions recruit —
  Chapter 1 (Articles 27–34) and Annex III of the Staff Regulations govern
  this. *(documented)* [4] [13]
- The **EPSO Competency Framework (2022)** defines 8 general competencies, each
  with observable "anchors": critical thinking/analysing & creative
  problem-solving; decision-making & getting results; information management
  (digital and data literacy); self-management; working together; learning as a
  skill; communication; intrapreneurship. *(documented)* [2]
- Since the **2023 reform**, EPSO competitions contain **no oral tests and no
  assessment centre**; selection runs on remotely proctored written tests, and
  general competencies are reviewed at **recruitment interviews run by the
  institutions** after the reserve list is published. *(documented)* [3]
- **Contract agents** (function groups I–IV) are selected differently: via the
  CAST database and computer-based reasoning plus competency MCQ tests, with
  interviews organised by the recruiting service, not EPSO. *(documented)*
  [7] [8]
- Under the pre-2023 model, EPSO's assessment centre included a **situational
  competency-based interview (SCBI)** built on a fictitious workplace
  assignment — official assignment booklets are still published and remain
  useful preparation material for institution-led interviews. *(documented)* [12]
- EU private-sector and multinational employers widely use structured,
  competency-based interviews; professional-body guidance (CIPD) recommends
  identical questions, pre-agreed scoring criteria and panel interviews to
  reduce bias. *(documented)* [16]
- For candidates: prepare STAR-format stories mapped to the 8 framework
  competencies — they are assessed at the recruitment interview even though
  they are no longer tested in the competition itself. *(guidance)*
- EPSO's process is under active change (new test platform, record 2026 AD5
  participation with roughly 3% reaching the reserve list) — always check the
  current Notice of Competition, the only official statement of what a given
  competition tests. *(documented)* [11] [17]

text:
This document answers, for both audiences:

- **Candidates** targeting the EU institutions (or the wider EU market): what
  competencies are on the table, at which stage they are assessed under the
  current EPSO model, and how contract-agent selection differs from official
  selection.
- **Interviewers and HR** in or around EU bodies: what the official framework
  and procedure documents actually say, and where interview-based assessment
  now sits (recruitment stage, not competition stage).

It is the orientation map for the EU Frameworks category. Deep dives — the
EPSO competency framework anchor-by-anchor, and EU assessment-centre / oral
test history and revival — are planned as child documents
(`epso-competency-framework`, `eu-institutions-assessment-centres`).

text:
Prefer the shared `GLOSSARY.md`; the following are used in the narrowed EU
sense in this document.

- **EPSO** — European Personnel Selection Office; the interinstitutional
  office that organises selection procedures for the EU institutions and
  bodies. Its careers portal is eu-careers.europa.eu (epso.europa.eu
  redirects there).
- **Open competition** — the selection procedure for permanent EU staff,
  announced by a Notice of Competition and ending in a reserve list [4].
- **Reserve list** — "a database containing information on the profile,
  competencies, and availability of all the successful candidates in a
  particular selection procedure", used by institutions to recruit [10].
- **AD / AST** — administrator and assistant function groups for permanent
  staff; graduates enter at AD5, specialists at AD6–AD9 or AST3–AST9 [4].
- **Contract agent / CAST** — contract staff selected via the "CAST Permanent"
  database in function groups I–IV, with EPSO-run reasoning tests and
  service-run interviews [7] [8].
- **Anchor** — an observable element of a general competency in the EPSO
  Competency Framework [2].
- **SCBI** — situational competency-based interview; the assignment-based
  interview used in EPSO assessment centres under the pre-2023 model [12].
- **Notice of Competition** — the official published document that specifies
  the tests and requirements of a given competition; EPSO's FAQ describes it
  as "the only official document that contains detailed information about the
  actual tests used by EPSO for a given competition" [11].
title: EU Competency Frameworks: EPSO & the EU Institutions

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

EPSO's Competency Framework is the EU-institution counterpart of national
schemes such as the UK Civil Service Success Profiles: it "defines the
competencies that are considered essential to perform effectively in any given
job, by the EU institutions and bodies that EPSO serves", and it explicitly
splits assessment between the two phases — "Some of those competencies are
assessed by EPSO during the selection phase, others are assessed by the EU
institutions and bodies themselves during the recruitment phase" [1].

The framework in force was introduced from 2022–2023, replacing the
eight-competency framework used since 2010; EPSO's 2023 information note on
the new competition model confirms "The general competency framework
(April 2022) remains valid" [1] [3]. The 8 general competencies, each defined
with observable anchors, are [2]:

| # | General competency (2022 framework) | Official one-line definition |
|---|-------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Critical thinking, analysing & creative problem-solving | "Thinks holistically and translates complex information into creative and innovative solutions." |
| 2 | Decision-making & getting results | "Takes decisions in a shifting environment, translates them into action and works towards tangible results." |
| 3 | Information management (digital and data literacy) | "Translates knowledge and skills through digital tools and technologies, optimises ways of working by utilising the digital environment." |
| 4 | Self-management | "Organises own tasks, demonstrates responsibility and a constructive attitude at all times." |
| 5 | Working together | "Demonstrates cross-functional cooperation and fosters diversity and inclusion. Cooperates well by seeking compromise, sharing knowledge and assisting others." |
| 6 | Learning as a skill | "Actively manages own learning, growth and self-improvement." |
| 7 | Communication | "Conveys information and opinions clearly, generates buy-in by facilitating interaction and engaging effectively with others." |
| 8 | Intrapreneurship | "Drives change, inspires and empowers others in implementing change and offers guidance at all levels." |

For orientation against the previous model: the pre-2023 assessment centre's
SCBI was "designed to assess the following competencies: Analysis & Problem
Solving, Learning & Development, Prioritising & Organising, Resilience,
Working with Others, and Leadership" [12] — readers will still meet these
older labels in preparation materials and older competition documents.

The legal frame sits above all of this: the Staff Regulations of Officials of
the European Union (Regulation No 31 (EEC), 11 (EAEC), consolidated) devote
Chapter 1 of Title III (Articles 27–34) to recruitment and Annex III to the
competition procedure, and the same instrument contains the Conditions of
Employment of Other Servants covering non-permanent staff [13].
title: EU Competency Frameworks: EPSO & the EU Institutions

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

## The current EPSO selection procedure for permanent officials

Permanent EU staff are "appointed for an indefinite period" and recruited
through open competitions that produce reserve lists. Two competition types
are published: **graduate competitions** (university graduates without work
experience, recruited as administrators at entry grade AD5) and **specialist
competitions** (graduates and non-graduates with field expertise, recruited at
AD6–AD9 or as assistants at AST3–AST9). Candidates apply via the Single
Candidate Portal; EPSO verifies eligibility (nationality, education,
languages); successful candidates join a reserve list, and "being on this
list means you are eligible for recruitment but not automatically
employed" [4].

The current competition model was set by EPSO's interinstitutional Management
Board on 31 January 2023. Its documented main features are [3]:

- "The 'Assessment Centre' and its related oral tests cease to exist.
  Therefore, oral tests will no longer be part of the selection procedures."
- Verbal, numerical and abstract/logical reasoning tests remain, but on a
  **pass/fail** basis.
- Generalist competitions (e.g. AD5) include an **EU knowledge test**;
  specialist competitions replace it with a **field-related MCQ** (ranked).
- All competitions include a **written test** (usually a case study) assessing
  drafting skills, corrected only for candidates with the best MCQ results.
- A **digital-skills test** may be included for some competitions.
- All tests are **online and remotely proctored**, in a single testing
  session, with a target timeline of six months from Notice of Competition to
  reserve list.
- Crucially for this knowledge base: "Candidates' general competences will
  still be reviewed via recruitment interviews, after the publication of
  reserve lists by EPSO", with EPSO offering the institutions recruitment-stage
  support including "evaluation of general competences"; "The choice of whom
  to recruit will remain entirely with the institutions."

The model is being exercised at scale: the 2026 AD5 generalist competition —
reported as the EU's first major recruitment drive of this kind after a
seven-year hiatus — expected 50,000–60,000 candidates (against 22,644 in the
last, 2019, AD5 round) for 1,490 reserve-list places, roughly a 3% success
rate, run fully digitally and remotely in all 24 EU languages, and including
the new digital-skills test [17]. The Notice of Competition remains "the only
official document that contains detailed information about the actual tests
used by EPSO for a given competition" [11].

## How the institutions assess competencies

Under the current model, interview-based competency assessment happens at the
**recruitment phase**: "The institutions check the profiles of the successful
candidates in the reserve list to identify those who best match their needs,
then they invite those selected for an interview", and candidates "may be
invited by several institutions or services" where lists are open to multiple
institutions [9]. Candidate CVs and competency data on the reserve list are
visible to recruiting institutions through the database [9]. Being listed is
no guarantee of a post, though EPSO's FAQ notes that on average around half of
listed candidates are recruited within seven months [10].

Under the pre-2023 model, the competency-based interview lived inside EPSO's
assessment centre as the **SCBI**: candidates received an official assignment
booklet describing a fictitious workplace scenario (e.g. an EU agency
preparing for the accession of three fictional Member States), were told the
content "is fictitious… derived from actual events, key details have been
changed", could annotate and keep the booklet during the interview, and were
assessed against named competencies with "No previous knowledge…
required" [12]. These booklets are still published on the EU Careers site and
show, in primary-source form, how EU-institution interviewers anchor
competency questions in a controlled scenario [12].

## Contract agents vs officials (survey level)

Contract staff are recruited through two routes: **CAST Permanent** — an
open-ended database in which applicants register and are shortlisted by
recruiting services when needs arise — and **ad hoc vacancies** with their own
procedures and deadlines [7]. The four function groups cover: "I. manual and
administrative support-service tasks; II. clerical or secretarial tasks,
office management and other equivalent tasks; III. executive tasks, drafting,
accountancy and other equivalent technical tasks; IV. administrative,
advisory, linguistic and equivalent technical tasks" [8].

Shortlisted CAST candidates sit computer-based multiple-choice tests: verbal,
numerical and abstract reasoning, plus a competency test (field knowledge) or
a language-comprehension test for linguist profiles [7]. The competency test
comprises 25 scenario-based MCQs (12 for translators/proofreaders) in the
candidate's second language, 50 minutes in total, one point per question with
no negative marking; pass marks are 13/25 for function groups II–III and
16/25 for function group IV [6]. Test results are valid for 10 years and for
the tested function group and any lower group; candidates who pass are then
invited to **recruitment interviews organised by the hiring services**, not by
EPSO [8]. Contracts are time-limited (initially 6–12 months, potentially
renewable), in contrast with officials' indefinite appointments [8]. The
Conditions of Employment of Other Servants, contained in the same consolidated
instrument as the Staff Regulations, provide the legal basis for these
non-permanent engagements [13].

## The wider EU market: private-sector competency interviewing

For candidates targeting the EU market beyond the institutions, structured
competency-based interviewing is standard professional practice rather than a
single published framework. CIPD guidance on selection identifies interviews,
psychometric tests, skill-based assessment tasks and assessment centres as the
main methods, strongly advocates structured interviews as "an effective way to
minimise bias and ensure there is a level playing field for candidates", and
describes their core elements: identical questions in the same order for all
candidates, pre-agreed scoring criteria, panel rather than sole interviewers,
and independent scoring before discussion [16]. Multinationals operating in
the EU typically publish their own values/competency models; the transferable
point is the method — structured questions scored against pre-defined
criteria — which this knowledge base treats in depth in
`techniques-foundation` and `best-practice-foundation`.
title: EU Competency Frameworks: EPSO & the EU Institutions

text:
Questions are grouped by use. Provenance: *(sourced …)* questions are
published by the named source; *(composed)* questions were written for this
knowledge base in the style of the cited EPSO Competency Framework (2022) [2]
and are **not** real EPSO or institution interview questions.

## EPSO-framework-style competency questions (recruitment-interview practice)

1. Give an example of when you had to analyse complex information to provide a creative and effective solution. *(sourced: Prepari.eu, "93 EU Job Interview Questions to Prepare For")* [18]
2. Tell me about a time when you had to act quickly without all the facts. What was the outcome? *(sourced: Prepari.eu)* [18]
3. Describe a situation where you faced an unexpected obstacle. How did you adapt? *(sourced: Prepari.eu)* [18]
4. Describe when collaboration was essential to achieve your goal. *(sourced: Prepari.eu)* [18]
5. Please describe a very stressful situation from your working life. *(sourced: Euphorum EPSO assessment-centre training guide, pre-2023 model)* [19]
6. Tell us about a time you had to judge the reliability of conflicting information from different digital sources before advising a colleague or stakeholder. *(composed — in the style of "Information management / digital and data literacy")*
7. Describe a piece of critical feedback that changed the way you work. What did you do differently afterwards? *(composed — in the style of "Learning as a skill")*
8. Give an example of a proposal you had to win support for from people holding different points of view. How did you generate buy-in? *(composed — in the style of "Communication")*
9. Tell us about a change you initiated in your team or organisation. How did you bring others along with it? *(composed — in the style of "Intrapreneurship")*
10. Describe a decision you took with incomplete information that did not work out as planned. What would you do differently now? *(composed — in the style of "Decision-making & getting results")*
11. Tell us about a period when your workload and priorities changed at short notice. How did you organise yourself and stay constructive? *(composed — in the style of "Self-management")*

## Motivation and EU-context questions (institution recruitment interviews)

1. Why do you want to work for the EU institutions — and why this institution in particular? *(composed)*
2. Which EU policy area interests you most, and how does your experience prepare you to contribute to it? *(composed)*

## Contract-agent-style questions (function groups III–IV)

1. Tell us about a time you had to draft a clear, accurate document under a fixed deadline. How did you assure its quality? *(composed)*
2. Describe a time you supported a team delivering a task outside your usual responsibilities. What did you contribute? *(composed — in the style of "Working together")*
title: EU Competency Frameworks: EPSO & the EU Institutions

text:
**Coaching layer — guidance, not documented fact.**

- **Map your stories to the 2022 framework.** Prepare two or three STAR
  (Situation–Task–Action–Result) stories per competency in the table above,
  using the anchors in the official leaflet [2] as your checklist of what to
  evidence — e.g. for "Working together", show compromise-seeking, knowledge
  sharing and inclusion, not just "we got on well". The STAR structure itself
  is standard structured-interview practice (see `techniques-foundation`).
- **Aim your interview preparation at the recruitment stage.** Under the
  current model there is no oral test in the competition; the interview that
  decides whether you convert a reserve-list place into a job is run by the
  recruiting institution [3] [9]. Prepare it like any structured competency
  interview, plus motivation and EU-context questions.
- **Practise in your second language.** CAST competency tests are taken in the
  candidate's second language [6], and institution interviews commonly probe
  language ability; rehearse your STAR stories in both of your declared
  languages.
- **Use the published SCBI booklets as rehearsal material.** They are official,
  free, and show the level of scenario complexity EU assessors have used [12];
  practise extracting priorities and stakeholder positions, then answering
  competency probes about the scenario.
- **Keep answers concise and evidenced.** A useful discipline is 1.5–2 minutes
  per STAR answer, with your personal contribution ("I", not "we")
  foregrounded — this is coaching consensus, not an EPSO rule.
title: EU Competency Frameworks: EPSO & the EU Institutions

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

- **What is scored at selection (current model).** Reasoning tests are
  pass/fail; the EU-knowledge or field MCQ is ranked; the written/case-study
  test is corrected only for the best MCQ scorers; eligibility is verified
  before listing [3]. In CAST procedures, published pass marks are explicit:
  13/25 (FG II–III) and 16/25 (FG IV), one point per question, no negative
  marking [6].
- **Who decides recruitment.** "The choice of whom to recruit will remain
  entirely with the institutions"; EPSO offers the recruiting services
  training, guidance, tools and "evaluation of general competences" at the
  recruitment stage [3]. Institutions shortlist from the reserve-list
  database — where candidate profiles and competencies are visible — and
  interview those who best match their needs [9].
- **How the legacy EPSO interview was run.** The SCBI was administered under
  controlled conditions ("may only be administered and interpreted by persons
  trained and authorised by EPSO"), against named competencies, with the
  scenario booklet available to the candidate throughout [12]. A practitioner
  guide to the pre-2023 assessment centre describes two EPSO assessors working
  from a pre-developed questionnaire and scoring responses on a 1–10
  scale [19] — practitioner evidence, not an official EPSO publication.
- **Professional-body standard for interview scoring.** CIPD guidance tells
  interviewers to ask identical questions in the same order, score against
  pre-agreed criteria, use panels, and have panel members score independently
  before discussing — noting that multiple interviewers in one interview
  produce fairer, more accurate results than multiple one-on-one
  interviews [16].
title: EU Competency Frameworks: EPSO & the EU Institutions

text:
**Coaching layer — guidance, not documented fact.**

- **Preparing for the wrong stage.** Candidates drill for an EPSO oral test
  that no longer exists, and under-prepare the institution recruitment
  interview where competencies are actually reviewed [3]. Interviewers
  hiring from reserve lists can make the mirror error: assuming EPSO has
  already interview-tested general competencies — it has not, since 2023.
- **Using the old competency labels.** Much free preparation material still
  teaches the 2010–2023 set (Analysis & Problem Solving, Resilience,
  Leadership, …) [12] [19]; answers mapped only to those labels miss the
  2022 framework's new emphases — digital/data literacy, learning,
  intrapreneurship [2].
- **Treating the reserve list as a job offer.** Listing is eligibility, not
  employment [4]; candidates who stop engaging (stale CV, unwatched EPSO
  account) miss short application windows [9].
- **Ignoring the Notice of Competition.** Tests vary by competition; the
  Notice is the only official specification [11]. Generic advice — including
  this document — never overrides it.
- **Composed-question confusion.** Practice questions labelled *(composed)*
  above are stylistic imitations; presenting them (or any coach's list) as
  "real EPSO questions" misleads candidates — EPSO's real instruments are
  controlled documents [12].
- **Interviewer drift in unstructured interviews.** For EU private-sector
  panels: departing from identical questions and pre-agreed criteria is the
  documented bias risk CIPD guidance warns against [16].
title: EU Competency Frameworks: EPSO & the EU Institutions

text:
**Coaching layer.**

1. **Framework mapping (60–90 min).** Download the official competency
   leaflet [2]. For each of the 8 competencies, write one STAR story and
   check it against every anchor; flag competencies where you have no story —
   that is your preparation gap.
2. **SCBI dry run (half day).** Take a published SCBI assignment booklet [12],
   spend 45 minutes extracting the problems, stakeholders and a schedule, then
   have a partner ask you questions 1–5 from the Question Bank about the
   scenario. Self-score against the anchors [2].
3. **Second-language rehearsal (30 min, repeatable).** Deliver two of your
   STAR stories in your second language; record and review for clarity and
   concision.
4. **Interviewer drill (for hiring panels).** Using the 8 competencies [2] and
   CIPD's structure rules [16], design a five-question interview with a
   pre-agreed 1–5 scoring rubric per question; pilot it on a colleague and
   compare independent scores before discussion.
5. **Current-state check (15 min).** Before any application, open the live
   Notice of Competition and the EU Careers testing pages [5] [11] and note
   what the specific competition tests — write down the stages so you are
   preparing against the current, not remembered, process.
title: EU Competency Frameworks: EPSO & the EU Institutions

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.** Signposts only; this is not legal
advice.

- **Staff Regulations.** Recruitment of EU officials is governed by Title III
  Chapter 1 (Articles 27–34) of the Staff Regulations, with the competition
  procedure laid down in Annex III; the same consolidated instrument contains
  the Conditions of Employment of Other Servants covering contract
  staff [13].
- **Equal treatment in the wider EU market.** Council Directive 2000/78/EC
  establishes a general framework for equal treatment in employment and
  occupation, prohibiting direct and indirect discrimination on grounds of
  religion or belief, disability, age and sexual orientation, and applies
  expressly to "conditions for access to employment… including selection
  criteria and recruitment conditions, whatever the branch of activity and at
  all levels of the professional hierarchy" [14] — i.e. it covers
  competency-interview design and scoring by EU employers.
- **Candidate data.** Processing of personal data by the EU institutions,
  bodies, offices and agencies — including candidates' selection data — falls
  under Regulation (EU) 2018/1725, which protects "persons whose personal data
  are processed by Union institutions and bodies in any context
  whatsoever" [15]. (Private-sector EU employers process candidate data under
  the GDPR; see the best-practice foundation document.)
- **Accessibility.** EPSO's published model emphasises online testing as "more
  accessible to the vast majority of candidates, wherever they are" [3];
  candidates needing adjustments should follow the procedure in the relevant
  Notice of Competition [11].
title: EU Competency Frameworks: EPSO & the EU Institutions

text:
- **EPSO is mid-transformation.** The 2023 model note, a new test platform and
  the record-scale 2026 AD5 competition [3] [17] show a process still being
  tuned; details (test mix, timelines, providers) can change per competition.
  This document describes the model as published at 2026-07-19; the Notice of
  Competition always prevails [11].
- **URL churn.** epso.europa.eu redirects to eu-careers.europa.eu, and several
  older EPSO pages now return 404; links here were verified on 2026-07-19 but
  the site is being reorganised.
- **Legacy materials dominate the preparation market.** Most freely available
  EPSO interview guidance (including two sources cited here [12] [19])
  describes the pre-2023 assessment centre; it remains useful for interview
  practice but does not describe the current competition phase.
- **Recruitment-interview practice varies by institution.** Official sources
  confirm that institutions interview from reserve lists and that recruitment
  choice is theirs [3] [9], but no single public document standardises how
  those interviews are structured or scored; statements here about their
  conduct are deliberately limited.
- **Private-sector coverage is directional.** The private-sector section
  states method-level facts from professional-body guidance [16], not claims
  about any named employer's process.

title: Interview Formats & Assessment Methods

text:
Competency questions do not arrive in one standard package. In current UK and
EU practice the same underlying question — "show us evidence of this
competency" — is delivered through several documented formats: one-to-one and
panel interviews, telephone screening calls, live and pre-recorded (asynchronous)
video interviews, and assessment centres in which a structured interview sits
alongside group exercises, presentations, in-tray/e-tray exercises and
situational judgement tests. The UK Civil Service documents both
competency-style *behaviour* questions (prepared examples of past actions) and
*strengths* questions (unrehearsed responses about what you do well and enjoy)
within the same Success Profiles framework; EPSO documents structured
competency-based and situational competency-based interviews within EU
selection procedures. Private-sector graduate recruitment leans heavily on
online assessments, on-demand video interviews and (often virtual) assessment
centres, as documented in named employers' public guidance and sector survey
reporting.

This is the Tier-1 foundation document for the Formats category: breadth over
depth. For each format it records what it is, who documents using it, and how
competency questions appear in it — for candidates preparing and for
interviewers weighing the documented pros, cons and fairness considerations of
each format. Per-format deep-dives (panel interviews, video/asynchronous
interviews, assessment-centre exercises) are planned child documents.

Confidence is **Medium**: definitions and process facts rest on official
framework, regulator and professional-body documentation, but claims about how
*prevalent* each format is rest partly on careers-service and sector-survey
reporting, and one EPSO assessment-centre source is an archived snapshot.
title: Interview Formats & Assessment Methods

text:
- The same competency can be probed in any format; what changes is the
  delivery, the time pressure, and who is watching. The UK Civil Service lists
  interviews, video interviews, assessment centres and job simulations as
  channels for assessing the same Success Profiles elements. *(documented)*
- The Civil Service documents a formal distinction between behaviour questions
  (structured, considered examples) and strengths questions (genuine first
  response, explicitly not to be rehearsed). *(documented)*
- EPSO documents structured competency-based interviews (about 10 minutes per
  competency) and situational competency-based interviews, and an Assessment
  Centre model in which at least two trained assessors observe every exercise.
  *(documented)*
- Graduate-scheme pipelines commonly run online assessment → on-demand video
  interview → assessment centre → final interview; PwC UK documents exactly
  this sequence publicly. *(documented)*
- ACAS advises more than one interviewer where possible to reduce
  discrimination risk, asking each applicant the same questions, and holding
  online interviews to the same standard as in-person ones. *(documented)*
- CIPD guidance is that structured interviews with predefined questions and
  consistent scoring minimise bias, and that panels scoring independently
  before discussion are fairer than lone interviewers. *(documented)*
- Sector reporting on the ISE Student Recruitment Survey 2025 highlights
  undisclosed candidate AI use during interviews as the most commonly reported
  form of assessment misconduct — a live integrity issue for remote formats.
  *(documented)*
- For candidates: prepare STAR-style evidence once, then adapt its delivery to
  the format — compressed for telephone and pre-recorded video, interactive for
  panels, distributed across exercises at an assessment centre. *(guidance)*

text:
This document answers two questions. For candidates: "the invitation says
telephone interview / video interview / assessment centre — what will the
competency questions look like there, and how should my preparation change?"
For interviewers and HR: "which formats are documented in UK and EU practice,
what does authoritative guidance say each is good and bad at, and what fairness
obligations attach to each?" It is the orientation layer for the Formats
category; child documents will go deeper on individual formats.
