text:
Covered: the documented formats competency interviews take in the UK and EU —
panel interviews; one-to-one structured interviews; telephone screening;
live and pre-recorded (asynchronous) video interviews; assessment centres and
the exercises that surround the interview within them (group exercises,
presentations, in-tray/e-tray, situational judgement tests); and the
strengths-based versus competency/behaviour-based distinction as documented in
Civil Service Success Profiles. UK Civil Service, EPSO and private-sector
graduate-scheme practice all receive visible weight.

Not covered here: question-answering technique (STAR etc. — see
techniques-foundation), competency-by-competency question banks (see
competencies-foundation), full framework descriptions (see
uk-frameworks-foundation and eu-frameworks-foundation), and per-format
deep-dives (planned child documents: panel-interviews,
video-asynchronous-interviews, assessment-centre-exercises). Technical,
case-study and academic interviews are out of scope except where they carry
competency questions.
title: Interview Formats & Assessment Methods

text:
Narrow, document-specific usages; see the shared glossary for general terms.

- **Panel interview** — an interview conducted by more than one interviewer at
  once, scoring the same candidate against the same criteria.
- **Structured interview** — an interview using a predefined set of questions,
  asked in the same order to all candidates, with responses scored against
  pre-agreed criteria.
- **Asynchronous (pre-recorded) video interview** — an interview with no live
  interviewer: questions are presented on screen and the candidate records
  timed answers on a platform for later review. Also called "on-demand" or
  "recorded" video interview.
- **Assessment centre** — a selection event (in person or virtual) combining
  several tasks — typically an interview plus exercises such as presentations,
  group exercises, written analyses or in-tray/e-tray simulations — observed
  by multiple assessors.
- **Strengths-based interview** — an interview probing what the candidate does
  regularly, does well and finds energising, expecting unrehearsed responses,
  as distinct from a competency/behaviour interview seeking prepared examples
  of past performance.
- **Telephone screening** — an early-stage interview by phone used to filter a
  large applicant pool before later-stage interviews or assessment centres.
title: Interview Formats & Assessment Methods

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

The UK Civil Service recruits against Success Profiles, which assess up to five
elements per role: behaviours, strengths, ability, experience and technical
skills [1]. The framework is format-plural by design: behaviours can be
assessed through application forms, judgement and personality tests, an
interview, an assessment centre, or job-related simulations including a
presentation, in-tray exercise, written analysis, group exercise, role-play and
oral briefing [2]. Strengths can be assessed through "interviews, recorded
video interviews, bespoke situational strengths test, simulation assessment,
personality test" [3]. The Civil Service Careers site tells applicants that
after applying they may face online tests, a video interview, a final
interview and an assessment centre, and that video interviews come in two
types: "a regular face-to-face interview in real-time via a video feed" and "a
pre-recorded interview where you will be asked to pre-record answers in
response to interview questions presented to you via an online platform" [4].

EPSO (the European Personnel Selection Office) documents two structured
interview instruments for EU-institution selection: the competency-based
interview (CBI), which evaluates selected general competencies with candidates
"interviewed for about 10 minutes per competency" using questions "structured
to allow the assessors to evaluate the candidate's selected general
competencies" [5], and the situational competency-based interview (SCBI), "an
interview that aims to assess general competencies by asking the candidate how
he/she would react in a specific situation" [6]. EPSO's Assessment Centre
model assesses general and/or specific competencies "by means of different
exercises, the content of which is validated by the selection board: a case
study, an oral presentation, a structured interview, an interview in the
field, an e-tray, etc.", with each exercise observed by at least two trained
assessors; per an archived EPSO FAQ, the non-case-study tests were "normally
held remotely" [7].

In UK private-sector and graduate-scheme practice, formats are documented in
employers' own public guidance: PwC UK's early-careers process runs online
assessment (behavioural test plus cognitive assessment), then an on-demand
video interview, then a virtual assessment centre, then a final "business
assessment" interview with a senior leader [8]. EY UK tells candidates their
"interview experience at EY may be a mix of in-person, video and phone
interviews" [9]. The Institute of Student Employers' Student Recruitment
Survey 2025 gathers selection-practice data from the UK's largest group of
early-careers employers [10]; reporting on it states it drew on 155 employers
who together handled over 1.8 million applications and hired more than 31,000
students [11].

| Format | Documented user (examples) |
|--------|---------------------------|
| One-to-one / panel interview | Civil Service final interview, 45–60 minutes, in person or via video [4]; ACAS advises more than one interviewer where possible [12] |
| Telephone screening | Documented by careers services as an early filter for large applicant pools [13][14] |
| Live video interview | Civil Service (real-time video feed) [4]; EY (mix including video) [9] |
| Pre-recorded (asynchronous) video interview | Civil Service (pre-recorded answers via online platform) [4]; PwC (on-demand video interview on the SHL platform) [8] |
| Assessment centre | Civil Service (combination of interview, presentation, written exercise, role-play) [4]; EPSO (case study, oral presentation, structured interview, e-tray) [7]; PwC (virtual assessment centre) [8] |
| Strengths-based interviewing | Civil Service Success Profiles Strengths element [3][4] |
| Structured competency interview | Civil Service behaviours [2]; EPSO CBI/SCBI [5][6]; CIPD-recommended practice [15] |
title: Interview Formats & Assessment Methods

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

## One-to-one and panel interviews

Face-to-face interviews — questioning by "an individual or panel of
interviewers" — remain the most common interview form according to Oxford
University Careers Service, typically lasting at least 30 minutes [14]. In
Civil Service recruitment the final interview "will usually be face-to-face in
person or via video", usually lasts 45–60 minutes, and "you may be asked to
describe specific occasions when you have demonstrated a certain skill or
behaviour" [4]. The same interview may also carry strengths questions: "You
may also be asked questions about what interests you and what you enjoy doing.
These questions will help the interview panel assess your strengths and
preferred style of working" [4]. A Civil Service Careers walkthrough of a
typical campaign describes being "interviewed by 3 people" who ask questions
on the strengths and the behaviours listed in the job advert [4]. ACAS advises
that "if possible, each interview should be carried out by more than 1 person
to reduce the risk of discriminating", and that interviewers should ask each
applicant the same questions where possible [12]. CIPD defines the structured
interview as asking "a predefined set of questions, in the same order, to all
candidates", with responses "scored using consistent criteria against a set of
pre-agreed criteria for each question" [15].

## Telephone screening

Careers-service guidance documents telephone interviews as an early-round
filter: they are "often used by employers in the first round of their
application process to filter large numbers of applicants", with successful
candidates progressing to a face-to-face interview or assessment centre
[14]. Prospects states that a member of the HR team usually conducts the
call at a pre-arranged time, that a telephone interview typically lasts
between 20 and 45 minutes, and that candidates "should expect the same
questions as in a face-to-face interview", listing behavioural examples such
as "Tell me about a time you worked in a team" [13]. Competency questions
therefore appear in this format in compressed, audio-only form.

## Video interviews — live and pre-recorded (asynchronous)

The Civil Service documents the two video-interview types quoted in Framework
Context above [4]. Prospects describes the live type as "similar to a regular
face-to-face interview" conducted over Microsoft Teams or Zoom with an
interviewer or panel in real time, and the pre-recorded type as answering
"pre-recorded or even written questions on screen" by recording an answer
"often to a time limit", which "helps employers who have lots of candidates,
as they can simply watch your answers at a time that suits them"; video
interviews are "often used as an early screening method" [16]. Oxford
University Careers Service notes pre-recorded video interviews "are used by
some large employers, particularly for applications to graduate schemes"
[14]. PwC UK documents its on-demand video interview in detail: it is
"conducted virtually via the SHL platform", with an expected duration of one
hour and a one-week completion window; "you will be presented with a series of
questions on the screen and asked to describe your experience. Instead of
having an interviewer, your answers will be recorded and reviewed afterwards"
[8]. The Success Profiles Strengths guidance explicitly lists "recorded video
interviews" among the ways strengths are assessed [3].

## Assessment centres

CIPD describes assessment centres as requiring candidates "to complete several
different tasks", often combining "behavioural ratings, cognitive and
personality assessments obtained from multiple sources", overseen by trained
selectors [15]. In Civil Service recruitment, "an assessment centre will
usually consist of a combination of different selection methods, such as an
interview, a presentation, a written exercise and/or a role-play exercise"
[4]. Oxford University Careers Service describes centres lasting a half or
full day, combining presentations, team exercises, written tests, interviews
and psychometric tests, and allowing employers "to compare the performance of
lots of candidates at the same time" [14]. PwC UK runs "a virtual immersive
assessment centre" at which the candidate works "with up to five other
candidates", supported by a facilitator [8]. EPSO's Assessment Centre
combines a case study, an oral presentation, a structured interview, an
interview in the field and an e-tray, as set out in the relevant notice of
competition, with candidates "observed/evaluated in a systematic way by at
least 2 trained assessors" during each exercise [7]. The competency interview
is thus one scored exercise among several: within Success Profiles, the same
behaviours may also be evidenced through job simulations such as group
exercises, in-tray exercises and role-plays [2], and CIPD reports that
skill-based assessments (work samples, situational judgement tests, simulation
exercises, assessment centres) "have been found to be better predictors of
performance on the job" than approaches such as unstructured interviews [15].

## Strengths-based versus competency/behaviour-based interviewing

Success Profiles documents both modes side by side. Behaviours are "the
actions and activities that people do which result in effective performance in
a job", and candidates "may be asked to give examples of when you have
demonstrated a particular behaviour" [2]. Strengths are "the things we do
regularly, do well and that motivate us", and the guidance instructs: "Do not
rehearse your answers - because we are looking for your initial response"
[3]. Civil Service Careers renders the contrast operationally: candidates
"answer the strengths questions with your genuine first response" and "answer
the behaviour questions with well structured and considered examples" [4].
Oxford University Careers Service adds that in strengths interviews assessors
attend to "not only what you say but also how you say it", looking for tone,
body language and expression demonstrating genuine motivation, so "there is no
real right or wrong answer" [14].

## Where the ISE survey sits

The ISE Student Recruitment Survey is the sector benchmark for UK
early-careers selection practice; the 2025 edition is available to ISE members
[10]. Public reporting by ISE's joint CEO records its scale (155 employers,
1.8 million applications, 31,000+ hires) and its findings on assessment
integrity in remote formats, discussed under Assessor Perspective below [11].
title: Interview Formats & Assessment Methods

text:
How the *same* competency — working with others / teamwork — is probed across
formats, plus the format-defining variants an interviewer would pair with it.
Sourced questions are published by the named source; composed questions were
written for this knowledge base in the style of the cited frameworks and are
not attributed to any employer.

## Telephone screening

1. Tell me about a time you worked in a team. *(sourced: Prospects, "Telephone interviews", June 2025)* [13]
2. Describe a time you had to solve a difficult problem. *(sourced: Prospects, "Telephone interviews", June 2025)* [13]
3. In no more than two minutes, describe the team you currently work in and the part you personally play in how it performs. *(composed)*

## One-to-one and panel competency interviews

1. Tell me about a time when you worked as part of a team to deliver a goal. *(sourced: Oxford University Careers Service, "Types of Interview")* [14]
2. Describe a situation in which you had to persuade someone round to your way of thinking; how did you use your communication skills to convince them? *(sourced: Oxford University Careers Service, "Types of Interview")* [14]
3. Tell me about a time when you demonstrated strong leadership skills. *(sourced: Oxford University Careers Service, "Types of Interview")* [14]
4. You said the team missed its first deadline — what did you, personally, do differently in the week that followed? *(composed — panel-style follow-up probe)*

## Strengths-based interviews

1. When are you at your best? *(sourced: Oxford University Careers Service, "Types of Interview")* [14]
2. How would a close friend describe you? *(sourced: Oxford University Careers Service, "Types of Interview")* [14]
3. How do you feel when working on a disorganised project? What do you do in situations like this? *(sourced: Oxford University Careers Service, "Types of Interview")* [14]
4. When a group task stalls, do you find yourself drawn to restarting the discussion or to quietly fixing the work itself? Which energises you more? *(composed)*

## Pre-recorded (asynchronous) video interviews

1. Describe a time you supported a struggling colleague while still meeting your own deadlines. You have 60 seconds to prepare and two minutes to record your answer. *(composed — timed on-demand format)*

## Assessment centres — structured and situational interview exercises

1. In the scenario pack you have just read, a colleague whose input you depend on has missed two handovers without explanation. How would you approach the situation, and what would you do if it continued? *(composed — in the style of EPSO's situational competency-based interview)*
2. Thinking back to this morning's group exercise: how did you make sure the quieter members of your group contributed to the final recommendation? *(composed — assessment-centre interview drawing on an observed exercise)*
title: Interview Formats & Assessment Methods

text:
**Coaching layer — guidance, not fact.**

- **Build the evidence once, deliver it per format.** The competency evidence
  underneath a good answer does not change between formats — what changes is
  packaging. Prepare a bank of STAR-structured examples mapped to the
  advertised criteria (see techniques-foundation), then rehearse three
  deliveries: a 90-second compressed version (telephone, pre-recorded video),
  a 3–4 minute interactive version that survives follow-up probing (panel),
  and a headline version you can attach to an exercise debrief (assessment
  centre).
- **Respect the behaviours/strengths boundary.** For behaviour questions the
  documented expectation is considered, structured examples; for strengths
  questions it is your genuine first response, explicitly not rehearsed
  [3][4]. Preparing for a strengths interview therefore means self-knowledge
  work — knowing honestly what energises you — not scripting answers. Forcing
  a STAR story into "When are you at your best?" answers the wrong question.
- **Pre-recorded video: answer the timer as well as the question.** With a
  visible time limit and no interviewer to redirect you, front-load the
  situation and your action; a recruiter reviewing recordings later [8] sees
  your first sentence for every question. Practise recording yourself —
  practitioner guidance stresses rehearsal precisely because the format feels
  unnatural [16].
- **Panels: answer the asker, include the room.** Direct your answer to the
  panel member who asked, glancing to the others; expect a different panellist
  to probe your example. If three interviewers each own different Success
  Profiles elements [4], expect the same story to be mined from different
  angles — do not repeat one example for every element.
- **Assessment centres: the interview is one data point you can steer.** Where
  a centre combines an interview with group exercises and simulations [4][7],
  assessors triangulate. Consistency matters more than any single star
  performance: claiming collaboration in the interview and dominating the
  group exercise is the visible failure mode.
- **Telephone: compensate for the missing channel.** With no visual feedback,
  signpost structure verbally ("the situation was… what I did was… the result
  was…") and keep answers inside a few minutes — screening calls are short and
  question-dense [13].
title: Interview Formats & Assessment Methods

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

- ACAS advises interviewing with more than one person where possible "to
  reduce the risk of discriminating", asking each applicant the same questions
  where possible, holding online interviews "in the same way as any interview
  you hold in person", obtaining consent before recording, and storing notes
  and recordings securely and confidentially [12].
- CIPD's selection guidance holds that structured interviews — predefined
  questions in a fixed order, scored against pre-agreed criteria — are "an
  effective way to minimise bias and ensure there is a level playing field for
  candidates from marginalised groups"; it further reports research findings
  that a panel interviewing together produces fairer and more accurate results
  than multiple one-to-one interviews (citing Huffcutt, Culbertson and
  Weyhrauch, 2013), and that interviewers should score independently before
  discussing candidates to avoid being influenced by senior colleagues (citing
  the Behavioural Insights Team, 2017) [15].
- CIPD also recommends combining methods — supporting interview insights with
  other data such as psychometric tests or task simulations — and reports that
  skill-based assessments out-predict unstructured interviews for job
  performance [15].
- EPSO's Assessment Centre model builds multi-assessor scoring in by design:
  every exercise is observed "in a systematic way by at least 2 trained
  assessors", exercise content is validated by the selection board, and final
  results rest on the evaluation of each competency across exercises [7]. Its
  interview instruments are explicitly structured: about 10 minutes of
  questioning per competency in the CBI [5], and situation-anchored
  questioning in the SCBI [6].
- Remote formats have a documented integrity problem. Reporting on the ISE
  Student Recruitment Survey 2025 records that undisclosed or unpermitted
  candidate AI use during interviews was the most commonly reported form of
  assessment misconduct (cited by 61% of respondents), that only 15% of
  employers reported never suspecting or identifying cheating, and that the
  share encountering cheating frequently doubled year on year from 7% to 15%
  [11]. PwC UK publicly documents counter-measures: periodic reviews
  "comparing candidate video interview responses, assessing if transcripts are
  identical and proactively looking for duplicate accounts", with
  disqualification where the process is undermined [8].
- A documented advantage of recorded formats for assessors: the recording can
  be re-watched rather than relying only on notes [16] — with the
  corresponding data-protection duties noted under Legal & Fairness below
  [12][17].
title: Interview Formats & Assessment Methods

text:
**Coaching layer — guidance, not fact.**

- **Candidates: treating every format as the same interview.** Reciting a
  rehearsed STAR script at a strengths interview collides with the documented
  instruction not to rehearse [3]; improvising at a behaviour interview wastes
  the documented invitation to bring considered examples [2][4].
- **Candidates: ignoring the clock in asynchronous video.** Recorded formats
  impose hard time limits [8][16]; answers that spend ninety seconds on
  scene-setting get cut off before the action and result.
- **Candidates: performing to the wrong audience at assessment centres.**
  Competing with other candidates in a group exercise reads badly when the
  competency being scored across exercises is collaborative [7][15].
- **Candidates: treating a telephone screen as informal.** It is a scored
  interview carrying the same competency questions as later rounds [13].
- **Interviewers: unstructured "chat" interviews.** Dropping the predefined
  question set and scoring criteria removes exactly the features CIPD
  identifies as bias-reducing [15], and diverging from consistent questions
  cuts against ACAS guidance on treating applicants the same way [12].
- **Interviewers: lone interviewing and groupthink scoring.** Interviewing
  alone where a second interviewer is feasible, or discussing candidates
  before independent scoring, both run against the cited guidance [12][15].
- **Interviewers: bolting a format on without adjusting for access.** Each
  format shifts the adjustment burden differently (technology, time limits,
  travel, group settings); the duty to make reasonable adjustments applies to
  the recruitment process, whatever the format [18][19].
title: Interview Formats & Assessment Methods

text:
**Coaching layer.**

1. **Format-transfer drill.** Take one teamwork example. Deliver it three
   ways this week: (a) recorded on your phone with a 2-minute cap and one
   retake maximum, (b) spoken aloud on a phone call to a friend who interrupts
   with two follow-up probes, (c) written as a 90-second exercise-debrief
   answer. Compare against the behaviours descriptors you are targeting [2].
2. **Strengths honesty pass.** Without preparation, answer aloud: "When are
   you at your best?" and "How do you feel when working on a disorganised
   project?" [14]. Then check: did your answer describe something you do
   regularly, do well and find motivating — the documented definition of a
   strength [3]?
3. **Mock panel.** Recruit two friends; give each a different competency and
   the same scoring sheet; have them score you independently before comparing
   notes, mirroring the independent-scoring practice CIPD describes [15].
4. **Tech rehearsal for video.** Practitioner guidance is unambiguous that
   platform, camera, lighting and timing should be tested days before, not on
   the day [16]. Do a full dress-rehearsal recording and review it.
5. **Interviewer drill: structure audit.** Before your next interview round,
   write the question set and scoring criteria down and fix the order; after
   the round, check what percentage of what you actually asked was on the
   sheet. Compare your practice against ACAS and CIPD guidance [12][15].
title: Interview Formats & Assessment Methods

text:
**Evidence layer — every claim cited.**

- **Reasonable adjustments.** Under the Equality Act 2010, section 20, the
  duty to make reasonable adjustments comprises three requirements: where a
  provision, criterion or practice, a physical feature, or the lack of an
  auxiliary aid puts a disabled person at a substantial disadvantage,
  reasonable steps must be taken to avoid the disadvantage or provide the aid
  [18]. GOV.UK guidance for employers gives interview-stage examples:
  allowing "wheelchair users to have their interview on the ground floor" and
  "candidates to complete a written test using a computer" [19]. ACAS
  guidance likewise expects employers to ask interviewees about adjustment
  needs, giving examples such as accessible venues, extra time for written
  tests, and a sign-language interpreter [12]. Every format in this document
  — telephone, video platform, timed recording, group exercise — is a
  "provision, criterion or practice" to which the duty can attach [18].
- **Consistency as fairness.** ACAS links asking each applicant the same
  questions to treating applicants in the same way [12]; CIPD links
  structure, panels and independent scoring to reduced bias and a "level
  playing field" [15].
- **Candidate data.** Interview notes and recordings are personal data:
  ACAS requires securely and confidentially stored notes and recordings, and
  explicit consent before recording an online interview [12]. The ICO's
  employment-practices guidance on recruitment and selection sets out how UK
  GDPR and the Data Protection Act 2018 apply across the whole recruitment
  process — from advertising through to deleting candidate information —
  including the handling of sensitive information such as health data, and
  distinguishes what employers *must* (law), *should* (expected practice) and
  *could* (options) do [17]. Recorded and platform-mediated formats
  (asynchronous video, virtual assessment centres) therefore carry documented
  data-protection obligations alongside their logistical advantages [17].
- **EU selection context.** EPSO's model embeds procedural fairness through
  structure: selection-board-validated exercise content and a minimum of two
  trained assessors per exercise [7].
title: Interview Formats & Assessment Methods

text:
- **Prevalence claims are the weak layer.** Which format is "most common" and
  how many employers use video interviews rests on careers-service and
  sector-survey reporting [11][13][14][16], not on official statistics; the
  underlying ISE 2025 survey report is member-only [10], so its figures are
  cited here through named secondary reporting.
- **EPSO sources are in flux.** EPSO's web estate has migrated (epso.europa.eu
  redirects to eu-careers.europa.eu), several legacy pages now 403/404 for
  automated tools, and the Assessment Centre FAQ cited here is a 2022
  Internet Archive snapshot [7]; EPSO's selection model has been undergoing
  reform, so the exercise mix for any given competition is governed by its
  notice of competition [7]. Treat competition-specific detail as
  time-sensitive.
- **Employer processes change yearly.** PwC's and EY's process pages [8][9]
  describe current cycles as of the access date; graduate-scheme stages,
  platforms and timings are routinely redesigned — reporting on the ISE 2025
  survey notes a third of employers had already redesigned selection processes
  in response to generative AI [11].
- **Named-employer detail is illustrative, not exhaustive.** PwC and EY are
  cited because their guidance is public and verifiable, not because they are
  the only or the typical users of these formats.
- **Bot-blocked source.** The CIPD factsheet [15] serves browsers but blocks
  automated link checkers; a human should verify it periodically.

- Reliable, citable UK prevalence statistics per format (e.g., what share of

  current notice of competition in the eu-frameworks deep-dive.
- Whether any UK regulator guidance specifically addresses algorithmic/AI
  scoring of recorded video interviews beyond the ICO's general recruitment
  guidance [17] — candidate for a best-practice child document.

title: Interview Best Practice: Preparation, Pitfalls & Scoring Insight

text:
This is the foundation document for the Best Practice category. It gathers what
authoritative UK and EU sources actually advise about preparing for, conducting
and scoring competency-based interviews — for **both** sides of the table. For
candidates, it covers the documented preparation guidance published by GOV.UK,
the National Careers Service, ACAS and named university careers services; how
to build a personal evidence bank mapped to the target framework; and what
published scoring guidance (such as the Civil Service 1–7 rating scale) reveals
about how answers earn marks. For interviewers, it covers the documented best
practice on structured questioning, consistent scoring, note-taking and legal
compliance published by ACAS, the CIPD and the ICO.

The document gives visible weight to private-sector and graduate-scheme
practice: the CIPD's guidance on structured selection applies across sectors,
and Institute of Student Employers (ISE) survey data shows why disciplined
preparation matters in a graduate market that now averages 140 applications per
vacancy. As a Tier-1 foundation it favours breadth over depth; dedicated
deep-dives (evidence bank construction, scoring and marking, mistakes and
recovery, interviewer question design, fairness and legal) are planned as
children of this document.

Confidence is **Medium**: the core claims rest on official and
professional-body sources (GOV.UK, COPFS, ACAS, CIPD, EPSO, legislation), but
the synthesis across sectors involves some inference, one key scoring source is
a single department's published articulation of the Success Profiles scale, and
the ICO recruitment guidance cited was published in draft for consultation.
title: Interview Best Practice: Preparation, Pitfalls & Scoring Insight

text:
- UK official guidance tells candidates to prepare **examples mapped to the
  advertised behaviours**: read the job advert, identify the behaviours or
  competencies listed, and prepare a concrete example for each. *(documented)*
- The **STAR structure** (Situation, Task, Action, Result) is the answer
  structure recommended by the National Careers Service and GOV.UK Success
  Profiles guidance, with CARL (Context, Actions, Results, Learning) a
  documented alternative. *(documented)*
- Where scoring guidance is published, competency answers are scored on
  **numeric scales against pre-agreed criteria** — e.g. the Success Profiles
  1–7 scale from "Not Demonstrated" to "Outstanding Demonstration", with 4
  commonly required as a minimum. *(documented)*
- Documented interviewer best practice: **more than one interviewer**, the
  **same questions for every applicant** where possible, decisions scored
  against the job description and person specification, and notes stored
  securely under data-protection law. *(documented)*
- Structured interviews — same questions, same order, responses scored against
  objective criteria — are professional-body best practice because they make
  candidates comparable and reduce bias. *(documented)*
- Graduate-scheme competition is at a record high (140 applications per
  vacancy on average across ISE employers), which is why careers services
  stress preparation and a varied evidence bank rather than improvisation.
  *(documented)*
- The most common failure mode on the candidate side is narrating the team's
  story instead of your own actions; on the interviewer side it is
  inconsistent questions and unscored, impressionistic judgements.
  *(guidance)*
- A reusable **evidence bank** of 6–10 STAR examples, each mapped to two or
  three competencies of the target framework, covers most first interviews and
  is the single highest-leverage preparation asset. *(guidance)*
title: Interview Best Practice: Preparation, Pitfalls & Scoring Insight

text:
This document answers two questions. For candidates: *"What do authoritative
sources actually tell me to do to prepare for a competency-based interview,
and how will my answers be scored?"* For interviewers and HR: *"What do ACAS,
the CIPD and the regulators say a well-run, defensible competency interview
looks like?"* It is the entry point to the Best Practice category and the
anchor for its planned deep-dive children.
title: Interview Best Practice: Preparation, Pitfalls & Scoring Insight

text:
**Covered:** documented preparation guidance from UK official careers sources
and named university careers services; the evidence-bank method of mapping
personal examples to a target framework; published scoring guidance
(Success Profiles-style numeric scales) and what it implies for answer
construction; documented interviewer best practice (structure, consistency,
note-taking, record-keeping); an overview of common pitfalls on both sides;
and the UK/EU legal guardrails at signpost level. UK Civil Service, EU
institution (EPSO), private-sector and graduate-scheme contexts are all in
scope, with the latter two given visible weight.

**Not covered (planned deep-dives):** step-by-step evidence-bank construction
(building-evidence-bank), detailed scoring and marking schemes across
employers (scoring-and-marking), mistake diagnosis and in-interview recovery
(common-mistakes-recovery), question-writing for interviewers
(interviewer-question-design), and full legal analysis
(fairness-bias-legal). Framework definitions themselves belong to the
Competencies, UK Frameworks and EU Frameworks categories; interview formats
(video, panel, assessment centre) belong to Formats; answer techniques in
depth belong to Techniques.
title: Interview Best Practice: Preparation, Pitfalls & Scoring Insight

text:
- **Evidence bank** — this knowledge base's term for a prepared, reusable set
  of real examples from work, study, volunteering or life, each written up in
  STAR form and mapped to the competencies of a target framework. The term is
  ours; the underlying practice is documented in the sources cited in Core
  Reference.
- **Structured interview** — an interview in which all candidates are asked a
  predefined set of questions, in the same order, with responses scored
  against pre-agreed criteria (see Assessor Perspective).
- **STAR** — Situation, Task, Action, Result: the answer structure recommended
  by the National Careers Service and Success Profiles guidance.
- **CARL** — Context, Actions, Results, Learning: an alternative structure
  documented by the University of Edinburgh Careers Service.
- **Benchmark** — a minimum score (per element or in total) that a recruiting
  panel sets for progression or appointment under Success Profiles-style
  scoring.
title: Interview Best Practice: Preparation, Pitfalls & Scoring Insight

text:
**Evidence layer — cited.** Best-practice guidance for competency interviews
comes from four distinct source families, each authoritative for a different
part of the picture:

| Source family | Authority for | Key sources |
|---|---|---|
| UK official (GOV.UK, Civil Service) | How the largest UK competency framework tells candidates to prepare and how it assesses | Success Profiles candidate overview [1]; Civil Service Behaviours [2]; COPFS assessment guidance [3] |
| UK public careers services | General candidate preparation advice | National Careers Service interview advice [4], STAR method [5], common questions [6] |
| EU official | EU-institution competency assessment | EPSO competency framework [7] |
| Professional & advisory bodies | Interviewer-side best practice, fairness, private-sector selection | ACAS interviewing guidance [11], ACAS discrimination guidance [12], CIPD selection methods [13] |

The UK Civil Service Success Profiles framework defines behaviours as "the
actions and activities that people do which result in effective performance in
a job", and lists nine of them (Seeing the Big Picture, Changing and
Improving, Making Effective Decisions, Leadership, Communicating and
Influencing, Working Together, Developing Self and Others, Managing a Quality
Service, Delivering at Pace); recruiting managers choose the subset best
suited to the role, so candidates never face all nine at once [2]. EPSO's
competency framework plays the equivalent role for EU institutions: it
"defines the competencies that are considered essential to perform effectively
in any given job" for the EU institutions and bodies EPSO serves, with each
competency assessed against defined observable elements called anchors — some
assessed by EPSO at selection, others by the recruiting institution [7].

For the private sector there is no single mandated framework, but the CIPD's
selection guidance documents the same structured, criteria-scored approach as
cross-sector professional best practice [13]. In the graduate market, ISE
survey data (155 employers, over 1.8 million applications handled) records
the competitive context this guidance operates in: applications per graduate
vacancy have risen from 38 in 2002/03 to 140 in each of the last two years,
with 79% of employers redesigning or reviewing recruitment processes in
response to AI developments [14]. University careers services (Oxford,
Edinburgh) and graduate careers platforms (Prospects) publish the most
detailed candidate-side preparation guidance and are treated here as named
practitioner sources [15] [16] [17] [18].
title: Interview Best Practice: Preparation, Pitfalls & Scoring Insight

text:
**Evidence layer — cited.**

## What official sources advise candidates to do

The Success Profiles candidate overview tells applicants to study the job
advert and prepare framework-mapped examples: "Think about examples you can
give of times when you have shown the behaviours outlined in the job advert"
[1]. It also advises reflecting in advance on "what you feel are your personal
strengths and preferred ways of working" for strengths-based elements, and
notes that interviews typically last 30–60 minutes and may be face-to-face,
by phone, by video or recorded [1]. Behaviours may be assessed via
application examples, tests, interviews, assessment centres or job
simulations, and candidates may draw examples from work, volunteering or
hobbies [2].

The National Careers Service's interview advice breaks preparation into
before, during and after. Before: check the interview format, "read the job
description carefully", research the employer's website, review your CV,
"prepare some examples from your past experience to refer to in the
interview", practise answers, and prepare your own questions. During: arrive
early, be ready 5–10 minutes before the start, ask the interviewer to repeat
or explain a question you do not understand, and structure answers with the
STAR method. After: ask when you will hear back, follow up if no timeline was
given, and ask for feedback if unsuccessful [4].

The STAR method itself is documented by the National Careers Service as:
Situation — "the situation you had to deal with"; Task — "the task you were
given to do"; Action — "the action you took"; Result — "what happened as a
result of your action and what you learned" [5]. The University of Edinburgh
Careers Service documents CARL as an alternative: "Context: Briefly set the
scene; Actions: What did you do?; Results: Briefly outline the outcome;
Learning: what did you learn from the experience" [17].

## Building a personal evidence bank mapped to the target framework

The mapping practice is documented consistently across sources. Prospects
advises candidates to "read and understand the job advert", then "from the
job description or person specification pick out the main competencies that
the employer is looking for and think of examples of when and how you've
demonstrated each of these", drawing "on a variety of experiences from your
studies, previous employment or any work experience", and warns against
overusing one example across a whole interview [18]. The University of Oxford
Careers Service advises preparing "a series of points in line with the job
description / person specification, stressing those aspects of your
experience, qualifications and skills that match the employer's requirements
most closely" rather than memorising scripted answers [16]. Edinburgh notes
that "Your application may form the basis of your interview so the
interviewer may ask you to elaborate or provide further examples", that
employers "expect interviewees to show evidence of commercial or business
awareness", and recommends practising aloud while avoiding over-rehearsal
[17]. The Success Profiles guidance anchors the same practice for Civil
Service applications: examples of times you have shown the advertised
behaviours, structured using STAR [1].

## The competitive context for graduates and career changers

ISE's Student Recruitment Survey 2025 (155 employers, 1.8 million
applications, 31,000+ hires) records: graduate hiring down 8% year-on-year;
an average of 140 applications per graduate vacancy for the second year
running (up from 38 in 2002/03), peaking at 290 for retail, FMCG and tourism
roles; 49% of employers permitting candidate AI use (mainly for CVs and cover
letters); and 61% of employers reporting undisclosed candidate AI use at
interview — driving process redesign at 79% of employers [14]. Despite the
volumes, 93% of employers report they can almost always or often recruit the
quality of graduate they need [14].
title: Interview Best Practice: Preparation, Pitfalls & Scoring Insight

text:
General and opening competency questions suitable for a first practice
session. Sourced questions are quoted from the named publication; composed
questions are written for this knowledge base in the style of the cited
frameworks and are not attributable to any employer.

## Opening questions from public careers guidance

1. When have you faced a challenging situation? *(sourced: National Careers Service)* [6]
2. Can you tell us about a personal achievement at work? *(sourced: National Careers Service)* [6]
3. Have you ever taken the initiative? *(sourced: National Careers Service)* [6]
4. Have you ever failed at a task? *(sourced: National Careers Service)* [6]
5. What are your main strengths? *(sourced: National Careers Service)* [6]

## Core competency starters from named careers services

1. Tell me about a time when you were part of an effective team. *(sourced: University of Oxford Careers Service)* [15]
2. Describe a situation when you assumed the role of leader. *(sourced: University of Oxford Careers Service)* [15]
3. Give an example of a time when you had to cope under pressure. *(sourced: University of Oxford Careers Service)* [15]
4. Tell me about a time when you have had to balance or juggle a number of conflicting priorities. *(sourced: University of Oxford Careers Service)* [15]
5. Give me an example of a time when you were particularly successful, or delivered over and above expectations. *(sourced: University of Oxford Careers Service)* [15]
6. Describe a situation in which you led a team. *(sourced: Prospects)* [18]
7. Give an example of a time you handled conflict in the workplace. *(sourced: Prospects)* [18]

## Composed first-session questions

 1. Tell me about a time you had to build a productive working relationship with someone whose priorities differed from your own. What did you do to make it work? *(composed)*
 2. Describe a time you had to make a decision without all the information you would have liked. How did you decide, and what was the outcome? *(composed)*
 3. Give an example of a time you explained something complicated to someone outside your specialism. How did you check they had understood? *(composed)*
title: Interview Best Practice: Preparation, Pitfalls & Scoring Insight

text:
**Coaching layer — this is guidance, not documented fact.**

- **Build the evidence bank first, not answers to individual questions.**
  Write up 6–10 real examples in STAR form [5], each two to three sentences
  per letter, and map each example to two or three competencies of the target
  framework (the nine Civil Service Behaviours [2], EPSO's general
  competencies [7], or the employer's person specification [18]). Most
  first-round questions — including all fifteen above — can then be answered
  by selecting and adapting, not inventing under pressure.
- **Spend most of the answer on Action, in the first person singular.**
  Scoring guidance rewards demonstrated behaviour (see Assessor Perspective):
  an answer that is mostly Situation, or that says "we" throughout, gives the
  assessor little positive evidence of *your* competence to score against the
  published scales [3]. A workable time split for a 2–3 minute answer:
  roughly 15% Situation and Task, 60–70% Action, the remainder Result and
  learning.
- **End with a result and, where natural, what you learned.** The National
  Careers Service's own definition of the R in STAR includes "what you
  learned" [5], and CARL makes learning an explicit fourth step [17] — a
  closing sentence on outcome plus reflection matches both documented
  structures.
- **Worked skeleton for question 6** (effective team, Oxford [15]):
  *Situation/Task* — one sentence naming the team, your role, and the goal at
  stake. *Action* — three or four first-person steps: how you took on your
  part, coordinated with others, handled a friction point. *Result* — what
  the team delivered, plus one thing you now do differently in teams.
- **Vary your sources of evidence.** Careers services advise drawing examples
  from study, work, volunteering and life, and warn that reusing one example
  across an interview reads as thin experience [18]; official guidance
  likewise accepts examples from work, volunteering or hobbies [2].
- **Practise aloud, but do not script.** Practising with another person while
  avoiding memorised answers is the documented advice from both Oxford and
  Edinburgh [16] [17]; scripts collapse under follow-up probes, which the
  published question sets explicitly include ("How did you make a
  contribution?", "What would you do differently?") [15].
title: Interview Best Practice: Preparation, Pitfalls & Scoring Insight

text:
**Evidence layer — cited.**

## Published scoring guidance

Where Success Profiles scoring guidance is published, behaviours are scored on
a 7-point scale. The Crown Office and Procurator Fiscal Service (a UK
government department recruiting under Success Profiles) publishes the scale
in full: "1 Not Demonstrated, 2 Minimal Demonstration, 3 Moderate
Demonstration, 4 Acceptable Demonstration, 5 Good Demonstration, 6 Strong
Demonstration, 7 Outstanding Demonstration", and states that at interview
"Behaviours, experience and technical skills are scored on the same 1-7
rating scale used at Sift" [3]. The same source documents pass and benchmark
mechanics: candidates "must demonstrate the minimum essential criteria for
all roles, this includes scoring 4 or more in all behaviours", with a single
3 tolerable where other scores are high, and boards may set higher
benchmarks — for example a 5 on lead behaviours or a total of 20 across
several behaviours — "where there is a job and business need" [3].

An example five-point competency evaluation scheme, reproduced by the
University of Oxford Careers Service from Civil Service resourcing
best-practice guidance, shows the underlying logic of evidence-based marking:
5 = "positive evidence of a high level of competence with no substantial
negative evidence" down to 1 = "little or no positive evidence of competence,
along with considerable negative evidence" [15]. In both schemes the assessor
scores the *evidence of behaviour in the answer* — positive and negative —
not the candidate's fluency or likeability.

## Documented interviewer best practice

ACAS guidance to employers on interviewing states that "each interview should
be carried out by more than 1 person to reduce the risk of discriminating",
that employers should "ask each applicant the same questions where possible…
to ensure you're treating applicants in the same way", that hiring decisions
should be based on how each applicant met each point in the job description
and person specification, and that interview notes or recordings must be
stored securely and confidentially in line with data-protection law (with
recording requiring the candidate's advance permission) [11]. The CIPD's
selection guidance defines the structured interview — a predefined set of
questions, asked in the same order to all candidates, with responses scored
against pre-agreed objective criteria — and documents its rationale: it makes
responses comparable and less likely that decisions rest on personal biases
[13]. The ICO's draft recruitment and selection guidance sets out employers'
UK GDPR / Data Protection Act 2018 obligations across the pipeline, including
sections on shortlisting, testing and interviewing candidates and on keeping
recruitment records [10]. At EU institutions, assessment against the EPSO
competency framework is anchored to defined observable elements per
competency, split between EPSO's selection phase and the recruiting
institution's own assessment [7].
title: Interview Best Practice: Preparation, Pitfalls & Scoring Insight

text:
**Coaching layer — this is guidance, not documented fact.** Each pitfall is
tied to the documented criterion it violates.

## Candidate-side

- **Narrating the team, not yourself.** Scales score demonstrated individual
  behaviour [3] [15]; an all-"we" answer leaves nothing to mark.
- **All situation, no action.** Long scene-setting starves the part of the
  answer where the published schemes look for positive evidence [15].
- **One example stretched across the whole interview** — explicitly warned
  against by Prospects as reading like limited experience [18].
- **Ignoring the advertised framework.** Official guidance says the behaviours
  to prepare for are listed in the job advert [1]; preparing generic answers
  instead of mapping to those behaviours misses the marking criteria the
  panel is scoring against.
- **Memorised scripts.** Documented follow-up probes ("What would you do
  differently?") [15] break scripted answers; careers services advise
  practising aloud without over-rehearsing [17].
- **No result, no learning.** Both documented structures end on outcome and
  learning [5] [17]; answers that trail off after the action forfeit that
  evidence.
- **Undisclosed AI assistance.** 61% of ISE employers report candidates using
  AI at interview without disclosure, and employers are actively redesigning
  processes in response [14] — treating AI as a drafting aid for preparation
  is different from relying on it live.

## Interviewer-side

- **Different questions for different candidates**, or scoring by overall
  impression rather than against pre-agreed criteria — the exact practices
  ACAS and CIPD guidance exist to prevent, because they make candidates
  incomparable and let bias in [11] [13].
- **Solo interviewing** where avoidable, against ACAS's more-than-one-person
  recommendation [11].
- **Missing, judgemental or over-retained notes.** Notes are disclosable
  personal data and must be stored securely with a records policy [10] [11];
  writing down evidence, not impressions, is the practice the scoring
  schemes assume [3].
- **Health or disability questions before offer**, or questions touching
  protected characteristics — prohibited territory covered in Legal &
  Fairness Considerations [8] [12].
title: Interview Best Practice: Preparation, Pitfalls & Scoring Insight

text:
**Coaching layer.** Drills a reader can run this week.

1. **Evidence-bank sprint (60–90 minutes).** List every substantial project,
   job, course, volunteering role and crisis of the last five years. Pick the
   eight richest. Write each up in four STAR sentences [5]. Map each to the
   competencies of one real target: the behaviours in a live Civil Service
   advert [1], EPSO's general competencies [7], or a private-sector person
   specification [18]. Gaps in the map tell you what experience to seek or
   which examples to develop next.
2. **Timed first-fifteen run.** Answer questions 1–15 above aloud, two to
   three minutes each, over two or three sittings. Record yourself; count the
   "we"-to-"I" ratio in each Action section and re-run any answer where "we"
   wins.
3. **Self-score against a published scale.** Re-listen to three answers and
   score each 1–7 using the published descriptors ("Acceptable
   Demonstration" = 4) [3], or the five-point positive/negative-evidence
   scheme [15]. Write one sentence per answer on what evidence was missing;
   revise the underlying evidence-bank entry, not just the answer.
4. **Mock panel with follow-ups.** Give a friend three sourced questions plus
   the documented probes ("How did you make a contribution?", "What would you
   do differently in future?") [15] and have them interrupt your scripted
   flow. Careers services document mock interviews and practising aloud as
   standard preparation [16] [17].
5. **Interviewer drill (for HR readers).** Take a live vacancy, draft five
   questions mapped to its person specification, agree scoring criteria per
   question before any interview, and run a paired mock scoring of one
   candidate answer — then compare scores and reconcile against the criteria,
   per ACAS and CIPD structured practice [11] [13].
title: Interview Best Practice: Preparation, Pitfalls & Scoring Insight

text:
**Evidence layer — cited. Signpost only; the fairness-bias-legal deep-dive
will treat these in full.**

- **UK — Equality Act 2010.** Discrimination against applicants in
  recruitment arrangements and hiring decisions is prohibited (s.39); the
  duty to make reasonable adjustments for disabled people is set out at
  ss.20–21; enquiries about disability and health before offer are restricted
  by s.60 [8]. ACAS's recruitment guidance operationalises this for
  interviews: the nine protected characteristics must not influence
  selection, "You must not ask a job applicant about their health or any
  disability at any stage in the application or interview process, unless an
  exception applies", and employers must make reasonable adjustments for
  disabled applicants and interviewees — for example accessible rooms,
  extended test time or alternative formats [12].
- **EU — equal treatment in recruitment.** Council Directive 2000/78/EC
  establishes the general framework for equal treatment in employment and
  occupation, and applies expressly to "conditions for access to employment…
  including selection criteria and recruitment conditions, whatever the
  branch of activity" (Art. 3(1)(a)), covering religion or belief,
  disability, age and sexual orientation [9].
- **Candidate data.** Interview notes, scores and recordings are personal
  data. The ICO's draft employment-practices guidance on recruitment and
  selection sets out employers' obligations under the UK GDPR and the Data
  Protection Act 2018, including for shortlisting, testing and interviewing
  and for keeping recruitment records [10]; ACAS likewise requires notes and
  recordings to be stored securely and confidentially, with recording only by
  advance permission [11].
title: Interview Best Practice: Preparation, Pitfalls & Scoring Insight

text:
- **The 1–7 scale citation is one department's published articulation.** COPFS
  publishes the Success Profiles 7-point scale and benchmark rules openly
  [3], and it matches practice described across Civil Service recruitment,
  but departments vary in benchmarks and in which elements they score; a
  central, GOV.UK-hosted scoring publication has not yet been verified for
  this document (see Open Questions).
- **The Oxford example-questions document is dated May 2016** [15]. Its
  questions and evaluation scheme remain live on the Careers Service site,
  but the Civil Service guidance it excerpts predates the current Success
  Profiles wording; treat its five-point scheme as illustrative of
  evidence-based marking logic, not as the current Civil Service instrument.
- **The ICO recruitment guidance cited is a draft** published for
  consultation (the page notes the final version is to follow) and references
  law amended by the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025 [10]; check for the final
  version at review.
- **CIPD pages bot-block automated link checkers**; the selection-methods
  factsheet URL is real and was verified in-browser terms via search excerpts
  [13], but a human should eyeball it periodically.
- **ISE full survey reports are member-gated.** All ISE figures cited here
  come from ISE's openly published summary of the 2025 Student Recruitment
  Survey [14], not the gated report.
- Private-sector practice varies far more than public-sector practice; CIPD
  guidance is best practice, not a mandate, and individual employers may run
  unstructured interviews that reward different behaviour.
title: Interview Best Practice: Preparation, Pitfalls & Scoring Insight

text:
- Is there a current, centrally hosted GOV.UK document publishing the
  interview evaluation form and 1–7 scale for general download? (A Civil
  Service Jobs interview evaluation form exists on
  assets.publishing.service.gov.uk but was not retrievable for verification
  during research; resolving this would strengthen the scoring section to
  High confidence.)
- How exactly are EPSO interview competencies scored (scale, pass marks,
  weighting) under the post-2022 competency framework? EPSO publishes the
  framework and anchors [7] but the scoring mechanics found so far sit in
  competition notices — a task for the scoring-and-marking deep-dive.
- What proportion of UK private-sector employers use fully structured,
  criteria-scored interviews versus semi-structured practice? CIPD documents
  the method [13] but current adoption data was not located.
- Whether the final ICO recruitment guidance (post-consultation) changes any
  obligation cited here [10].
title: Interview Best Practice: Preparation, Pitfalls & Scoring Insight
